86afa2ea-8e43-4458-96fc-7a12b6b6dec1.titulo: What a video technician/VJ does at live events
86afa2ea-8e43-4458-96fc-7a12b6b6dec1.conteudo_rico:
To start: Before you touch a single cable, you need to understand what is being delivered to the audience and what your responsibility is in the chain.

The live video technician is the professional who ensures that the right image reaches the right screen, at the right moment and in the right quality. Unlike the post-production editor (who works offline, with time to spare), the live video technician operates in real time: there is no second chance. In this lesson you'll understand the scope of the role, the positions that exist within an operation (VJ, switcher/vision mixer, media server operator, LED operator, signal engineer) and how they work together in a real event, from a church service to an arena show.

  • VJ (Video Jockey): A professional who creates and manipulates visual content in real time, usually synced to the music, using software such as Resolume Arena. It is the visual equivalent of the DJ: mixing clips, applying effects and reacting to the environment. Works a lot at parties, raves, electronic shows and stage scenography.
  • Switcher operator (switcher/TD): Also called the Technical Director in broadcast. Receives several sources (cameras, slides, videos) and decides which one goes to the main screen and to the LED wall, making cuts and transitions. It is the central role in conferences, church services, broadcasts and corporate events.
  • Media server / playback operator: Responsible for playing back pre-produced content (openers, stings, lower-thirds, stage backgrounds) reliably and in sync. Uses servers such as Watchout, Resolume, Modulo Pi, disguise (d3) or PlaybackPro.
  • LED operator / signal engineer: Handles the physical LED wall: configures the LED processor, maps the modules, ensures resolution and color calibration. On large structures, it is its own specialty.
  • Difference between live and post-production: In post you render, review and redo. Live, everything is processed and displayed in fractions of a second (low latency is critical). The mistake goes on air instantly, which is why redundancy, rehearsal and a checklist are mandatory.
  • Signal flow: This is the path the image travels: source (camera/computer) → converter/distributor → switcher/media server → processor → screen/projector. Understanding this chain is the foundation of all problem diagnosis.
  • Typical duties of the role: Live video mixing, mapping, effects and transitions, integration with audio (timecode/SMPTE), MIDI and OSC control, support for multiple screens, compatibility with various sources and hardware, and flexibility to reconfigure quickly in the field.

Equipment / details

  • Operating laptop/PC: A powerful machine (i7/i9 or Ryzen 7/9, 32 GB RAM, dedicated GPU) running the VJ or playback software. It is the modern operator's 'console'.
  • Video switcher (e.g., Blackmagic ATEM, Roland V-60HD): A vision-mixing console that receives multiple sources and delivers the final program. Used by the switcher operator.
  • Preview/program monitor: Reference screens for the operator to see what is on air (Program) and what comes next (Preview), avoiding cutting 'blind'.

Step by step

  1. 1. Map the event's roles: Ask the producer: will there be live cameras? An LED wall or projection? Pre-produced content? This defines how many roles and which equipment will be needed.
  2. 2. Draw the signal flow on paper: List all sources (PCs, cameras, players) and all destinations (LED wall, projector, recording, streaming). Connect them with arrows. This diagram becomes your setup map.
  3. 3. Define the working resolution: Choose the event's common resolution (e.g., 1920x1080@60) and make sure every source and destination supports it, to avoid rescaling and delays.

In practice: At a medium-sized church service, the switcher operator sits at a Roland V-60HD receiving 3 cameras and the lyrics/slides computer; they cut between cameras during worship and bring up the lyrics on the LED wall at the right moment. The wall is a P3 LED fed by a Novastar processor. Understanding that this slides computer is just one more 'source' in the chain completely changes how you diagnose a black screen.

Summary

  • The live video technician delivers the right image, on the right screen, in real time, with no second chance.
  • There are distinct roles: VJ, switcher operator, media server operator and LED operator.
  • It all starts with understanding the signal flow — source, processing and destination.

The Video Technician/VJ at Live Events

The video technician is responsible for the entire visual signal chain at an event: from camera to display, passing through the switcher, the playback server and the graphics. They operate the video switcher to choose, in real time, which source is on air — camera, VT, animation or black screen — control transitions and maintain the visual continuity of the show. The creative VJ extends this role by generating and manipulating visual content live, synced to the music or the script. In both cases, preparation begins hours before the show: checking cables and resolution, loading the playlist, calibrating projectors and running a technical rehearsal with the whole team. Precise, fast communication with the director, the camera operators and the stage crew is as essential as any technical skill.

fe816937-1c38-4c2b-92d2-f03af6cfb138.titulo: Resolution, frame rate, pixels and aspect ratio
fe816937-1c38-4c2b-92d2-f03af6cfb138.conteudo_rico:
To start: Every digital image is a grid of pixels refreshed N times per second. Mastering these numbers avoids 90% of cropped, stretched or shaky-screen problems.

Before operating any equipment, you need to speak the language of video: what resolution is, what frame rate is, what progressive and interlaced are, what aspect ratio is, and why mixing 50 Hz with 60 Hz gives you a headache. This lesson builds the numerical foundation that supports everything that comes after.

  • Pixel: The smallest unit of a digital image — a colored dot. The image is a matrix of pixels (width x height). More pixels = more detail, more processing load.
  • Resolution: Number of pixels horizontal x vertical. Common standards: HD 1280x720, Full HD 1920x1080, 4K UHD 3840x2160. In LED, the real resolution depends on how many panels you join together (e.g., 10 panels of 128x128 px = 1280 px wide).
  • Frame rate: How many images per second are displayed: 24, 25, 30, 50, 60 fps. Cinema is 24; TV in Brazil has historically been 30/60 (60 Hz standard); Europe 25/50. Higher frame rate = smoother motion.
  • Progressive (p) vs Interlaced (i): In progressive (1080p) each frame is drawn in full. In interlaced (1080i) each frame is split into two fields (odd and even lines). For LED and VJ, always prefer progressive — interlaced causes jaggies in motion.
  • Aspect ratio: The width:height proportion. 16:9 is the widescreen standard. LED walls often have 'crazy' proportions (e.g., 21:9, 32:9 or vertical 9:16 panels) — you need to produce content in the exact proportion of the wall so it doesn't distort.
  • Native resolution vs scaling: Every display has a native resolution. Sending a different signal forces the equipment to rescale (scaling), which adds latency and can blur. The ideal is always to match the output to the destination's native resolution.
  • Refresh rate in LED: Unlike the content's frame rate, this is the frequency at which the LED panel 'flashes' internally (e.g., 1920 Hz, 3840 Hz). A high refresh avoids the scan-line effect (black bands) when the screen is filmed by a camera.

Equipment / details

  • Bars/test pattern generator: Equipment or software that generates patterns (SMPTE bars, grid, circles) to check a screen's resolution, proportion and color before the event.
  • Scaler/processor (e.g., Analog Way, Barco): Converts an input resolution to the destination's native one, matching frame rate. Essential when sources and screens don't match.

Step by step

  1. 1. Find out the destination's native resolution: Count the LED panels or read the projector/monitor spec sheet. E.g., a wall of 12x6 cabinets of 168x168 px → 2016x1008 px.
  2. 2. Define the content canvas resolution: Produce the content at exactly that resolution to map pixel for pixel, with no rescaling.
  3. 3. Lock a single frame rate: Choose 50 or 60 Hz for the entire event and set all sources and processors to it, avoiding tearing and stutters.
  4. 4. Verify with a test pattern: Send a grid and a circle to the screen: if the circle becomes an ellipse, the proportion is wrong; if the grid doesn't line up with the edges, there is rescaling.

In practice: A client delivers an opening video in 4K 24p, but the LED wall is 2016x1008 running at 60 Hz. If you simply throw the file at it, the processor rescales and converts 24→60, potentially causing judder (irregular motion). The professional re-renders the content at the exact resolution of the wall and at 60p, eliminating the problem at the source.

Summary

  • Resolution is the pixel grid; frame rate is how many times per second it refreshes.
  • Always match the output to the destination's native resolution to avoid rescaling and latency.
  • Lock a single progressive frame rate for the entire event.

Resolution, Frame Rate and Aspect Ratio in Professional Video

Resolution is the number of pixels in width × height of an image; the more pixels, the more detail — but also the higher the bitrate and infrastructure demand. Frame rate (fps) determines motion fluidity: 24 fps evokes cinema, 30 fps is the NTSC/streaming standard, and 60+ fps is used in sports and live streams. Aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9) defines the geometric proportion of the image and must be compatible between the camera, the switcher and the destination screen — mismatches cause black bars or distortion. On LED panels, the pixel pitch defines the videowall's native resolution and the minimum comfortable viewing distance, directly connecting the physical design to the content specification.

0d50ba2c-29f5-40a2-809c-7e3467852c18.titulo: Building the video workstation (PC for VJ/playback)
0d50ba2c-29f5-40a2-809c-7e3467852c18.conteudo_rico:
To start: The VJ's computer is not an ordinary PC: it has to pump out pixels in real time without choking. Knowing how to spec it is what separates a smooth show from a laggy one.

Software like Resolume, Watchout and TouchDesigner are voracious for GPU, RAM and fast disks. This lesson breaks down each component, why it matters for live video and how to size the machine for the scale of the event — from a small party to a 4-screen 4K projection.

  • Processor (CPU): Latest-generation multicore (Intel Core i7/i9 or AMD Ryzen 7/9). It decodes video, manages effects and controls the system. For many simultaneous layers, more cores help.
  • Graphics card (GPU): The most critical component for VJing. NVIDIA GeForce RTX (e.g., RTX 4070/4080) or the professional line (RTX A-series/older Quadro) accelerate playback, effects and multiple outputs. The GPU defines how many layers and what resolution you can run without dropping FPS.
  • RAM memory: Minimum 32 GB for professional work. High-resolution video and many loaded clips consume a lot of RAM; too little memory causes stutters and bottlenecks.
  • SSD/NVMe storage: A fast NVMe SSD for the system and active media — 4K video demands high read rates (hundreds of MB/s to GB/s). Use an HDD only for archiving, never for live playback.
  • Video outputs and number of displays: Check how many outputs (HDMI/DisplayPort) the GPU has. For multiple screens/projectors, you can use a GPU with multiple outputs or an expander such as Datapath FX4/Matrox to split one output into several regions.
  • Cooling and power supply: Under full load the machine heats up; efficient cooling avoids throttling (performance drops). A high-quality power supply ensures stable power — instability crashes a show.
  • Connectivity and network: Thunderbolt/USB for capture cards and controllers; a fast network (Gigabit) for Art-Net/sACN (LED control over the network), NDI (video over the network) and media transfer.

Equipment / details

  • VJ laptop (e.g., laptop with RTX 4080, 32-64 GB, NVMe): A portable solution for parties and shows; watch out for overheating and a limited number of outputs.
  • Desktop workstation (e.g., tower with RTX A5000/4090): For multichannel projection and a home-built media server; more outputs, better cooling, more stable.
  • Datapath FX4 / Matrox TripleHead: An expander that takes one GPU output and splits it into up to 4 monitors/projectors as a single desktop — a classic for multi-screen mapping.
  • Capture card (e.g., Blackmagic DeckLink/UltraStudio, Magewell): Brings an external signal (camera, another PC) into the software as a live source.

Step by step

  1. 1. Size it by the load: Count simultaneous layers, resolution and number of outputs. The bigger it is, the more GPU/RAM/SSD you need.
  2. 2. Prioritize GPU and disk: For VJing, a strong GPU and a fast NVMe matter more than a top-of-the-line CPU.
  3. 3. Check the physical outputs: Make sure there are enough outputs (or an expander) for all the event's screens.
  4. 4. Test under real load: Before the event, run the full composition for 30+ minutes while watching FPS and temperature — simulate the worst case.
  5. 5. Disable updates and power saving: In the OS, turn off automatic updates, sleep mode and power saving that could interrupt the show.

In practice: For an electronic show with Resolume running 6 layers of 1080p clips + effects, outputting to a P3 wall, the operator uses an RTX 4080 laptop with 64 GB and NVMe. They turn off Wi-Fi, set the power plan to 'high performance', and monitor the FPS in the corner of the Resolume screen — if it drops below 60, they reduce the output resolution or simplify effects before it crashes on stage.

Summary

  • For VJ/playback, the GPU and the NVMe disk are the most critical components.
  • Practical minimum: i7/Ryzen 7, 32 GB RAM, NVMe and a dedicated GPU.
  • Always test under real load and disable anything that could interrupt the show.

VJ/Playback Workstation

The workstation for VJ and playback is the heart of the live video operation. The hardware must be sized to the show's resolution and frame rate: GPUs with hardware decoding (NVENC/NVDEC) and NVMe SSDs are indispensable for playing back HAP or ProRes files at 4K/60fps without dropped frames. The operating system also requires configuration — power plan in High Performance mode, updates suspended and antivirus with exclusions — to ensure no background process interferes during the event. Video outputs must be planned by interface standard (HDMI 2.0, DisplayPort 1.4, SDI) and validated against available bandwidth before the soundcheck.

1e38a69a-2351-4d7d-ad41-27f278f9e49e.titulo: Cables, connectors and connections: HDMI, SDI, fiber and DisplayPort
1e38a69a-2351-4d7d-ad41-27f278f9e49e.conteudo_rico:
To start: A show doesn't fail for lack of talent — it fails because of a wrong cable, one that's too long or poorly seated. Knowing connections is survival knowledge.

Each type of connection has a distance, bandwidth and robustness limit. This lesson covers the main professional video connections, when to use each one, and why SDI and fiber dominate the stage while HDMI lives in the corners.

  • HDMI: The ubiquitous consumer connector. Carries video + audio. Versions: 1.4 (up to 4K30), 2.0 (4K60), 2.1 (4K120/8K). Weak point: distance — the signal degrades quickly after ~5-10 m without an active cable or extender. Fragile connector, no lock.
  • SDI (Serial Digital Interface): The professional/broadcast standard over coaxial cable with a BNC connector (which locks). HD-SDI (1.5 Gbps, up to 1080i/p), 3G-SDI (1080p60), 6G/12G-SDI (4K). Advantage: runs 100+ m on coaxial, firm connector. It is the standard for professional cameras and switchers.
  • DisplayPort: A high-bandwidth computer connection, common on GPUs. Supports 4K and multiple screens (MST). Its weak point is also distance. Widely used between PC and processor/expander.
  • Fiber optic: For long distances (hundreds of meters to km) and environments with interference, you convert the signal (SDI/HDMI) to fiber. Immune to electrical noise, lightweight. The standard in arenas and large stages.
  • DVI and VGA: DVI is digital (a legacy of old monitors/processors); VGA is analog (blue, 15 pins) — dying out, but still shows up on old equipment. Avoid VGA when possible due to quality loss.
  • RJ45 / Cat (Ethernet): A network cable used for signals over IP: Art-Net and sACN (LED control), NDI (video over the network) and HDBaseT (video+control over a network cable up to ~100 m). Versatile and cheap for distance.
  • Physical care: Always leave slack and a strain-relief loop on connectors; lock BNC and screw down DVI/VGA; identify each cable with tape/labels; keep spare cables. A cable that's too long = weak signal; a cable that's too thin = loss.

Equipment / details

  • SDI cable (Belden coaxial + BNC): The stage standard for cameras and switchers; runs long distances with a locking connector.
  • Active / optical HDMI cable: For HDMI beyond ~10 m, use an active cable or HDMI over fiber so you don't lose signal.
  • SDI↔HDMI converter (e.g., Blackmagic Mini Converter): A bridge between the broadcast world (SDI) and HDMI equipment (projectors, consumer monitors).
  • Fiber transmitter/receiver (e.g., Blackmagic, AJA): Converts SDI/HDMI to optical for long distances in arenas.
  • HDBaseT extender (TX/RX pair): Carries HDMI over Cat cable up to ~100 m with a pair of boxes; common in corporate.

Step by step

  1. 1. Choose the connection by distance: Short (<5 m) and consumer equipment: HDMI. Stage and cameras: SDI. Very far or with interference: fiber. Over the network: HDBaseT/NDI.
  2. 2. Check the version/bandwidth: For 4K60 confirm HDMI 2.0+, 3G/6G/12G-SDI or a suitable DisplayPort — an old version limits resolution.
  3. 3. Convert when necessary: Use SDI↔HDMI converters to bridge different worlds; don't force passive adapters where an active converter is needed.
  4. 4. Lock, relieve and label: Lock BNC, give HDMI a strain-relief loop, label each end (source→destination).
  5. 5. Have redundancy: Bring spare cables and, at critical points, run a secondary path.

In practice: In an arena, the camera at the back of the audience is 80 m from the switcher. HDMI won't reach there. The technician runs SDI from the camera to a fiber converter near the stage, sends it over fiber to the PA/back, and converts it back to SDI at the ATEM switcher. On the LED wall, the signal comes out of the processor over short HDMI. Every cable choice is dictated by distance and environment.

Summary

  • HDMI for short runs and consumer gear; SDI/BNC for stage and cameras; fiber for long distances.
  • Always check the version/bandwidth — it limits the maximum resolution.
  • Lock, relieve, label and bring spares: most failures are physical.

b7a403fa-e75c-4240-8988-bf6949592d06.titulo: Converters, distributors, splitters, scalers and capture
b7a403fa-e75c-4240-8988-bf6949592d06.conteudo_rico:
To start: Between the source and the screen there is an army of little boxes that convert, split, match and capture signal. Knowing them is what makes any setup possible.

A source rarely connects directly to the destination without help. This lesson introduces the essential support boxes — converters, DAs, splitters, switches/matrices, scalers and capture cards — explaining the exact function of each and when you'll need them.

  • Converter: Changes the type of signal: SDI↔HDMI, HDMI→fiber, analog→digital. It doesn't change resolution — only the physical 'language' of the signal. E.g., Blackmagic Mini Converters.
  • Video distributor (DA - Distribution Amplifier / Splitter): Takes one source and replicates it to several identical outputs (1 in, 4-8 out). Used to send the same content to multiple walls/monitors. Warning: a cheap HDMI splitter can fail the HDCP handshake.
  • Video switch / matrix: Allows routing N inputs to M outputs (matrix). Unlike a splitter (which copies), the matrix chooses which source goes to which destination, without mixing/cutting with a transition — it's routing.
  • Scaler / processor: Converts the input's resolution and frame rate to the destination's native one, matching incompatible signals. Indispensable when the source doesn't match the screen. E.g., Analog Way, Barco, and LED processors already do scaling.
  • Capture card/device: Turns an external video signal (camera, console, another PC) into a source inside the software. Internal (PCIe, e.g., DeckLink) or external (USB/Thunderbolt, e.g., Magewell, UltraStudio).
  • Adapters: Change only the physical connector (e.g., mini-HDMI→HDMI, USB-C→HDMI). Passive ones only work when the signals are electrically compatible; otherwise, an active converter is needed.
  • HDCP and handshake: Protected content (Blu-ray, streaming) requires HDCP. Professional equipment often doesn't pass HDCP, causing a black screen. Be aware that your material/source must be free of HDCP to flow through the professional chain.

Equipment / details

  • Blackmagic Mini Converter / Teranex: A family of SDI↔HDMI converters, up/down/cross conversion and scaling — essential pocket boxes.
  • HDMI/SDI distributor (DA): Replicates one source to several identical screens; in SDI it is very reliable, in HDMI beware of HDCP.
  • Video matrix (e.g., 8x8 HDMI/SDI): Routes any input to any output; useful in corporate with several rooms/screens.
  • Scaler (Analog Way Pulse/Barco): Matches disparate resolutions and frame rates, with transition effects on some models.
  • Capture card (Blackmagic DeckLink / Magewell USB): Brings cameras and PCs into the VJ/streaming software as live sources.

Step by step

  1. 1. Identify the signal problem: Is it a different language (SDI vs HDMI)? Do you need to replicate? Do you need to route? Do you need to match resolution? Each problem calls for a different box.
  2. 2. Convert the language with a converter: Use an active converter for SDI↔HDMI or for fiber; don't rely on a passive adapter here.
  3. 3. Replicate with a DA / split with a splitter: For the same image on several screens, use a distributor; confirm HDCP in HDMI.
  4. 4. Route with a matrix if there are several sources and destinations: When the client wants to 'send PC1 to room A and the camera to room B', it's a matrix.
  5. 5. Match resolution with a scaler: If the source is 1080p and the screen is 2016x1008, the scaler/processor adjusts without distorting.
  6. 6. Capture what comes from outside: To bring a camera/console into the software, use a capture card compatible with the resolution.

In practice: A corporate event has 1 slides PC that needs to appear on 3 identical LED walls and in a recording. The technician passes the PC through a converter to SDI, into a 1x4 distributor: three outputs go to the walls' processors and the fourth goes into a capture card to record/broadcast. All from a single source, with no mixing.

Summary

  • A converter changes the signal's language; a splitter/DA replicates; a matrix routes; a scaler matches resolution.
  • A passive adapter only changes the connector — it does not convert incompatible signals.
  • Capture brings external cameras and PCs into the software as live sources.

Video Signal Distribution Infrastructure

At professional events, the video signal rarely goes straight from the camera to the destination. Splitters and distributors duplicate the signal to multiple destinations simultaneously without loss; converters adapt incompatible connectors (HDMI ↔ SDI); scalers adjust resolution and frame rate so that sources and destinations operate at the same specification. Capture devices allow the live signal to be recorded or streamed via USB or network. Each piece of equipment inserted into the chain adds latency and can limit the maximum resolution — knowing these constraints is essential to build a functional, artifact-free patch.

afbd3452-6272-4f3c-82b3-acc06ca9e63e.titulo: Display technologies: LCD, LED, OLED and choosing by application
afbd3452-6272-4f3c-82b3-acc06ca9e63e.conteudo_rico:
To start: Each screen works physically in a different way, and that changes brightness, contrast, angle and where it can be used. Understanding the technology avoids wrong choices.

The video technician needs to know not only how to operate but how to recommend the right screen. This lesson runs through the main display technologies, their advantages and limits, focusing on what matters for a live event: brightness, contrast, viewing angle and indoor vs outdoor use.

  • LCD: Liquid crystals modulate the light of a backlight (usually LED). Good color and contrast, affordable cost. At events it appears as monitors, support TVs and signage panels. Limit: insufficient brightness for strong sun and bezels when building a videowall.
  • LED (emissive panel): Each point is formed by LEDs that emit their own light — it is the modern stage wall. Very high brightness (1,000 to 5,000+ nits), modular (you can build any size/shape), no bezel between modules. Dominates shows, churches and arenas.
  • OLED: Each pixel emits its own light, with absolute blacks and vibrant colors; thin and even flexible screens. Common in premium TVs and phones; at events it appears less due to cost and size, but is excellent for refined scenography.
  • Plasma and other legacy tech: Plasma had good color and angle, but high consumption and wear; practically extinct. E-ink, transparent and curved are niches (shop windows, scenography, signage).
  • Brightness (nits) and environment: Indoor screens need less brightness; direct sun requires high-brightness LED (outdoor 5,000+ nits). Insufficient brightness 'washes out' the image in bright light.
  • Viewing angle and uniformity: At events the audience sees from many angles; LED and OLED keep color; LCD can lose contrast at the sides. Color uniformity between modules requires calibration.
  • LCD vs LED videowall: An LCD videowall has thin but visible bezels (black lines); LED has no visible seams, which is why it's preferred for large continuous imagery.

Equipment / details

  • Indoor LED panel (e.g., pixel pitch P2/P2.5/P3): For indoor stage and close cameras; smaller pitch = more resolution per meter.
  • Outdoor LED panel (e.g., P4/P6+, high brightness/IP): For outdoors, with rain sealing and high brightness for the sun.
  • Professional LCD monitor/TV: Stage support, confidence monitor, signage; cheap and simple.
  • OLED display: Premium scenography and booths where deep black and thinness matter.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define indoor or outdoor: Outdoor/sun → high-brightness, waterproof (IP) LED. Indoor → smaller pitch and moderate brightness.
  2. 2. Calculate the viewing distance: The closer the audience, the smaller the pixel pitch needed so they don't 'see the dots'.
  3. 3. Decide continuous vs informative imagery: Large continuous image → seamless LED. Isolated informative panels → LCD may suffice.
  4. 4. Consider a camera in the venue: If the wall will be filmed, require LED with a high refresh to avoid black bands in the capture.

In practice: For an indoor church service where the camera films the pastor with the wall behind, you choose P3 LED with a refresh above 1920 Hz: the pitch ensures good resolution for the audience's distance, and the high refresh prevents the camera from capturing scan lines on the wall behind him.

Summary

  • Emissive LED dominates the stage for brightness, modularity and the absence of seams.
  • LCD is cheap support; OLED is premium scenography; plasma is extinct.
  • Pixel pitch, brightness and refresh depend on distance, light and the presence of a camera.

Display Technologies: LCD, LED and OLED

LCD (Liquid Crystal Display) uses a backlight and liquid-crystal filters — good value for indoor environments with moderate light. Direct LED does away with the traditional backlight: each pixel is an LED, allowing brightness of up to 10,000 nits and unrestricted outdoor use. OLED (Organic LED) goes further: each pixel emits its own light and can be switched off individually, producing absolute black and infinite contrast — ideal for broadcasting and premium displays in dark environments. The right choice depends on ambient light, viewing distance, the pixel pitch required and the available budget.

f62fc874-4863-4615-9181-70c5897a6998.titulo: Anatomy of the LED wall: pixel pitch, processors and assembly
f62fc874-4863-4615-9181-70c5897a6998.conteudo_rico:
To start: Building an LED wall is like assembling a jigsaw puzzle that has to become a single perfect image. Here you'll learn each piece and the real step by step.

This is the most hands-on lesson of the module. You'll understand pixel pitch, modules and cabinets, the role of the LED processor/controller (Novastar, Colorlight), the sending and receiving boxes, and you'll go through the step by step of assembling and configuring a wall from scratch — from the resolution calculation to calibration.

  • Pixel pitch (P2, P3, P4...): The distance in millimeters between the centers of two neighboring LEDs. P3 = 3 mm. Smaller pitch = more pixels per meter = more resolution and cost. Indoor: P1.5-P3; outdoor/far: P4-P10.
  • Module and cabinet: LED comes in small modules mounted inside cabinets that fit together and lock. E.g., a 500x500 mm cabinet. You stack cabinets in width x height to form the wall.
  • Total wall resolution: The sum of the cabinets' pixels. E.g., a 500x500 mm cabinet with P3.91 pitch has 128x128 px; 10x5 cabinets = 1280x640 px.
  • LED processor / controller: The 'brain' that receives the video (HDMI/SDI/DVI) and distributes it to the cabinets via network cables, scaling to the exact resolution of the wall. Brands: Novastar (VX/MX series), Colorlight. It handles scaling, brightness, gamma and mapping.
  • Sending card and receiving card: Within the chain: the sending card (in the processor) sends the data; each cabinet has a receiving card that drives its LEDs. The configuration ('screen mapping') tells the system the order and position of each cabinet.
  • Data and power cabling: Cabinets connect in a daisy chain via network cables for data, and via power cables. There is a limit on how many cabinets per network port (depends on the cabinet's resolution) — which is why the wall is divided into columns/ports.
  • Calibration and uniformity: After assembly, brightness and color are calibrated so that all modules look the same (no lighter/darker 'patches'). Processors and software (e.g., NovaLCT) do this calibration.
  • Brightness, gamma and curve for camera: Brightness is adjusted to the environment and, if there is a camera, brightness/gamma are controlled so the wall doesn't blow out in the captured image.

Equipment / details

  • Novastar processor (e.g., VX4S, VX600, MX series): An all-in-one controller: takes HDMI/SDI/DVI, scales and sends to the cabinets; adjusts brightness/color. The market standard.
  • Colorlight processor: A popular alternative controller/sending unit for LED, with its own mapping software.
  • NovaLCT / SmartLCT software: Novastar's application for mapping cabinets, configuring receiving cards and calibrating the wall.
  • Indoor LED cabinets P2.5/P3.91: Cabinets that fit together and lock, forming the wall; choose the pitch by distance.
  • Cat (network) cables and PowerCon: Cat for data between cabinets/processor; PowerCon/power to feed each column.

Step by step

  1. 1. Calculate the wall: Define width x height in cabinets and the total resolution (multiply pixels per cabinet). Confirm the desired proportion.
  2. 2. Build the structure and the cabinets: Raise the structure (ground support or rigging), hang/fit the cabinets locking them, leveling so the surface is flat.
  3. 3. Connect data and power: Link cabinets in a chain via network cable respecting the per-port limit; distribute power across columns with load headroom.
  4. 4. Configure the processor: Feed the video into the processor, set the input resolution and the output resolution = the wall's native resolution.
  5. 5. Map the screen in the software: In NovaLCT/SmartLCT, enter how many cabinets in width/height and the cabling order, so the content appears in the right position.
  6. 6. Calibrate brightness and color: Run the calibration to make the modules uniform; adjust brightness to the environment and gamma if there is a camera.
  7. 7. Test with a test pattern: Send a grid and bars: check for dead modules, cabinets out of order, uneven color and edges.

In practice: For a 6x3 m stage-backdrop wall with 500x500 mm P3.91 cabinets (128x128 px each): that's 12x6 cabinets = 1536x768 px. The technician builds the structure, fits the 72 cabinets, runs data in columns of 6 cabinets per network port, configures the Novastar with 1536x768 output, maps it in NovaLCT in the cabling order and calibrates. In the end, a test pattern reveals one cabinet with the order swapped — fix it in the map and the image becomes perfect.

Summary

  • Pixel pitch and the number of cabinets define the resolution and quality of the wall.
  • The processor (Novastar/Colorlight) scales and distributes the video; sending/receiving cards drive the cabinets.
  • Correct mapping in the software and color/brightness calibration turn the wall into a single image.

Pixel Pitch, Processors and LED Wall Assembly

The pixel pitch (in mm) defines the distance between the centers of two adjacent LEDs and directly determines the resolution and the ideal minimum viewing distance: the smaller the pitch, the denser the image and the closer the viewer can be without perceiving individual pixels. The video processor (Colorlight, Novastar, Brompton, Linsn) is the brain of the system — it receives the signal from the source, converts resolution, manages colors (sRGB, Rec.709, HDR) and sends the image data to each cabinet via data cable. The cabinets (usually 500×500mm in the events market) are the physical modular units that form the panel: they are installed in grids and connected in a chain; a failure in a single cabinet interrupts the data run of that branch, hence the importance of spares in stock. Correctly calculating pitch × audience distance × area × power consumption is the first step of any video mapping or LED scenography project.

9d5f181a-170a-4593-919d-5a119a3b25d1.titulo: Projection: types, projectors, lumens and image calculation
9d5f181a-170a-4593-919d-5a119a3b25d1.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Projecting well means balancing light, distance and surface. Getting the lumens or the lens wrong ruins the image even with great content.

Projection is still essential in mapping, sets and environments where LED doesn't fit or isn't suitable. This lesson covers the types of projection, projector technologies (DLP, LCD, laser), the concept of lumens, throw ratio (distance vs. size) and the step by step to size a projected image.

  • Front vs. rear projection: Front: projector in front of the screen (most common, but it creates a shadow if someone walks by). Rear: projector behind a translucent screen, no shadow and a clean look — great for stage and scenography.
  • Projection distance (throw): Short, ultra-short and long distance. Ultra-short projects right up against the wall (no shadow, good for tight spaces); long distance throws from afar. The 'throw ratio' relates distance to image width.
  • Mapped (mapping), immersive and panoramic projection: Mapping projects onto 3D/irregular surfaces with alignment software. Panoramic/immersive uses several projectors with blend (smooth mixing at the edges) to cover large surfaces.
  • Technologies: DLP, LCD and laser: DLP uses micro-mirrors (good contrast, robust); 3LCD uses three panels (vivid colors). Light source: lamp (cheap, periodic replacement) or laser (more stable brightness, long life, quick on/off) — laser is today's professional standard.
  • Lumens (brightness): Measures luminous flux. The more ambient light and the larger the image, the more lumens you need. Small dark room: a few thousand; stage with lights and a large screen: 10,000-30,000+ lumens.
  • Lenses and zoom: Professional projectors have interchangeable lenses (short, standard, tele) and lens shift (moves the image without moving the projector). The right lens lets you place the projector wherever you can and still cover the screen.
  • Keystone and blending: Keystone corrects trapezoidal distortion when the projector isn't perpendicular (use sparingly — it degrades). Blending smooths the seam between projectors in a single large image.

Equipment / details

  • Professional laser projector (e.g., Epson, Panasonic, Barco, 10k-30k lumens): High brightness and stability for stage and mapping; interchangeable lens and lens shift.
  • Interchangeable lens (short/standard/tele): Chosen by throw ratio to cover the screen from the projector's possible position.
  • Front/rear projection screen: Reflective front or translucent rear; the screen gain and type affect brightness and angle.
  • Media server / mapping software (Resolume, MadMapper, Watchout): Does warp, mask and blend to align the projection to the surface and join projectors.

Step by step

  1. 1. Measure the screen and the environment: Define the desired image width and how much ambient light there is — this dictates the lumens.
  2. 2. Calculate the lumens needed: Larger image + brighter environment = more lumens. Add a margin so it doesn't look 'washed out'.
  3. 3. Define the distance and the lens (throw ratio): With the possible distance and the image width, choose the right lens (short/standard/tele) using the manufacturer's throw ratio.
  4. 4. Position and focus: Set up the projector, use lens shift/zoom to frame without keystone if possible; focus with a grid test pattern.
  5. 5. Do mapping/blend if needed: In the software, adjust warp and mask to the surface; on multi-projector, blend the edges.
  6. 6. Calibrate brightness/color: Match brightness and color between projectors and to the environment; check with a real image.

In practice: To project behind a stage onto an 8 m wide rear screen, with some stage light, the technician chooses a laser projector of ~20,000 lumens, calculates from the throw ratio that a short lens is needed because the space behind is only 6 m, positions it, focuses with a grid and uses lens shift to center it without keystone. Rear projection eliminates the shadows of performers passing in front.

Summary

  • Lumens and environment go together: more light and a larger screen require more brightness.
  • Throw ratio and lenses define where you can cover the screen from; lens shift avoids keystone.
  • Rear projection eliminates shadows; mapping and blend join surfaces and projectors.

Projection: Types, Lumens and Image Calculation

Choosing the right projector depends on three main variables: the light source technology (DLP, LCD, Laser), the brightness in ANSI lumens and the ratio between distance and image size (throw ratio). In lit environments, the lumen demand grows in proportion to the projection area and the location's illuminance level — a stage with windows may require ten times more lumens than a pitch-dark auditorium. The simulator lets you calculate in real time whether the selected projector meets the project, considering the projection screen gain as an amplification factor for perceived brightness.

015f5acc-0d96-43ca-920c-1184115b904e.titulo: Switchers: ATEM and Roland in practice
015f5acc-0d96-43ca-920c-1184115b904e.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The switcher is the heart of live operation: it's where you decide, in real time, what the world sees. Mastering the cut is mastering the event.

This lesson teaches the concept and hands-on operation of a video switcher: Program and Preview buses, cut, dissolve, wipe, keyer (chroma/luma), PIP, and the workflows of the most common consoles — Blackmagic ATEM and Roland V-60HD/V-800HD. You finish knowing how to set up and cut an event.

  • What a switcher is: A console that receives several sources (cameras, PCs, players) and selects/mixes which one goes to the program output (what goes on air). It replaces plugging/unplugging cables with an instant, clean switch.
  • Program and Preview (buses): Program (PGM) is what's on air now. Preview (PVW/PST) is what you prepare to come next. You select the next source in Preview and 'take' it on air with a cut or transition — you never cut blind.
  • Cut, Dissolve (Mix) and Wipe: Cut = instant switch (hard cut). Dissolve/Mix = smooth transition with a fade between sources. Wipe = one source 'pushes/sweeps' the other with a geometric shape. The T-bar controls transitions manually.
  • Keyer: chroma key and luma key: The keyer overlays one source on another by removing a color (chroma, e.g., green background) or by luminance (luma, e.g., a white logo on black). Used for captions, logos and characters over a background.
  • PIP (Picture-in-Picture) and DSK: PIP places a smaller image over another (e.g., a camera in the corner). DSK (Downstream Keyer) overlays graphics/lower-thirds 'on top of everything', without affecting the main transitions.
  • Multiview and tally: Multiview shows all sources + PGM/PVW on a single monitor for the operator. Tally is the little red light that comes on the camera that's on air, warning the camera operator.
  • Macros and AUX: Macros record sequences of actions to repeat with one button. AUX outputs deliver a specific signal (e.g., just the big screen, without the streaming graphics).
  • ATEM vs. Roland (philosophies): ATEM (Blackmagic) is widely used in streaming/churches, controlled by a physical panel or by the ATEM Software Control. Roland (V-60HD/V-800HD) is robust, with many physical controls and per-input scaling, popular in corporate and live events.

Equipment / details

  • Blackmagic ATEM Mini Pro / Extreme: Compact HDMI switcher with built-in streaming, multiview and DSK; great for churches and live streams. Controlled by software or panel.
  • Blackmagic ATEM Television Studio / Constellation: Larger SDI switchers for broadcast productions with more inputs and keyers.
  • Roland V-60HD: HD switcher with per-input scaling, 6 inputs (HDMI/SDI), PinP, key and physical controls — a live-event classic.
  • Roland V-800HD MK II: Larger switcher for big events, multilayer and robust processing.
  • Multiview monitor: A screen showing all sources + PGM/PVW so the operator can cut safely.

Step by step

  1. 1. Connect and label the sources: Plug cameras/PCs into the inputs; name each input (CAM1, SLIDES, VT) on the switcher so you don't get lost.
  2. 2. Match resolution and frame rate: Set the switcher format (e.g., 1080p60) and make sure all sources match; on the Roland, use the per-input scaler for different sources.
  3. 3. Configure the program output: Set the PGM output to the big screen/recording/stream; configure AUX if the big screen needs a different cut from the stream.
  4. 4. Prepare keys and DSK: Set up chroma/luma key if there is one; load lower-thirds/logo into the DSK to overlay without disturbing the cut.
  5. 5. Rehearse the cut: Select the next source in Preview and take it on air with a cut or dissolve via the T-bar; watch the tally and the multiview.
  6. 6. Record macros for repeated moves: For fixed sequences (e.g., open lyrics + name DSK), record a macro and trigger it with one button.

In practice: At a service, the operator on a Roland V-60HD has CAM1 (wide), CAM2 (pastor), CAM3 (audience) and the lyrics PC. He keeps CAM2 on PGM during the preaching, prepares CAM3 in Preview and does a smooth dissolve to the audience during a moment of applause, then overlays the Bible reference via DSK. Everything is guided by the multiview, with the tally telling the camera operators which camera is on air.

Summary

  • Program is what's on air; Preview is what you prepare — never cut blind.
  • Cut, dissolve and wipe are the transitions; keyer and DSK overlay logos and graphics.
  • ATEM shines in streaming/church; Roland is robust with scaling and physical controls.

Switchers: ATEM and Roland in Practice

A video switcher (or vision mixer) is the central piece of equipment in any live production: it determines which signal goes on air (the PROGRAM bus) while the operator prepares the next source on the PREVIEW bus. The transition between them can be a hard cut (CUT), a mix (MIX/dissolve), a dip to black (DIP) or a wipe (WIPE), each with precise timing controlled by the T-BAR or the AUTO button. Beyond the main switching, features like DSK (downstream key for graphics) and Chroma Key expand the visual layer without interrupting the on-air signal. Equipment such as the Blackmagic ATEM and the Roland V-series follows exactly this operating workflow.

54cd80d5-8c82-4c15-99a1-e411c851ec62.titulo: VJ in Resolume Arena: layers, clips, composition and effects
54cd80d5-8c82-4c15-99a1-e411c851ec62.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Resolume is the VJ's instrument. Whoever masters its logic of layers and clips plays visuals live like playing a keyboard.

This is the core lesson of software VJing. You'll learn the Resolume Arena interface, the logic of Layers and Clips, how to build a composition, apply effects and transitions, sync with the music (BPM), and map the output to the big screen. It's a practical operating manual.

  • Interface and structure: Resolume is organized as Composition → Layers (stacked like in Photoshop) → Clips (each cell is a video/image). The top layer overlays the ones below according to the blend mode and opacity.
  • Layers: Each layer plays one clip at a time. Stack layers to combine backgrounds + elements + text. The blend mode (Add, Screen, Multiply, Alpha) and opacity define how the layer mixes with the ones below.
  • Clips and Decks: Clips are the cells with media; clicking triggers the clip in that layer. Decks are pages of clips — you organize sets into decks (e.g., Deck 'Opening', Deck 'Drop'). Switching decks swaps the entire set of clips.
  • Transitions and Dashboard: Switching between clips in a layer can have a fade/transition. Clip parameters (speed, position, scale) are in the panel; the Dashboard exposes quick controls.
  • Effects (FX): Drag effects onto clips, layers or the whole composition (e.g., blur, glow, RGB shift, kaleidoscope, glitch). Effect parameters can be animated or linked to audio/BPM.
  • Sync with music (BPM): Set the BPM (manually or by tap) and the speed of clips/effects follows the music's tempo. Audio FX react to the audio in real time, making the visuals 'pulse' with the beat.
  • Output and mapping: In Advanced Output you define the output resolution, slice the composition and map it to the big screen/projector (including warp/mask). It's where the composition becomes a signal for the physical destination.
  • Control via MIDI/OSC: Map buttons/knobs of a MIDI controller (e.g., APC40, Akai) to trigger clips and adjust parameters without using the mouse — essential for playing live with your hands.

Equipment / details

  • Resolume Arena (software): The full version with Advanced Output (mapping, slicing, soft-edge); Avenue is the version without advanced mapping.
  • MIDI controller (e.g., Akai APC40 MkII, Novation Launchpad): A grid of buttons to trigger clips and knobs for effects — the VJ's tactile 'console'.
  • Capture card (e.g., Magewell/Blackmagic): Brings a camera/external feed in as a live source inside Resolume.
  • Strong dedicated GPU + NVMe: Needed for several high-resolution layers without dropping FPS.

Step by step

  1. 1. Create the composition at the destination resolution: In Composition Settings, set the resolution equal to the big screen's (e.g., 1536x768) to map without rescaling.
  2. 2. Import and organize clips into decks: Drag videos into the clips; organize them by show moment into decks (Opening, Drop, Closing).
  3. 3. Build the layers: Background layer at the bottom, elements and text above; adjust blend (Add/Screen) and opacity to compose.
  4. 4. Set the BPM and turn on Audio FX: Tap the BPM with the music; enable audio-reactive effects so the visuals pulse on the beat.
  5. 5. Apply effects: Drag FX onto clips/layers; animate parameters or link them to the BPM (e.g., strobe on the chorus).
  6. 6. Configure the output (Advanced Output): Define the output screen, slice and map it to the big screen/projector; apply warp/mask if the surface requires it.
  7. 7. Map the MIDI controller: Enter MIDI map mode, click the clip/parameter and move the corresponding button/knob to bind them.
  8. 8. Rehearse triggering clips live: Play the whole sequence with the controller, watching the FPS and the output on the preview screen.

In practice: For a DJ set, the VJ creates a 1920x1080 composition, builds a 'Build-up' deck with geometric loops in the background and a particle layer in Add mode on top. He taps the BPM and adds an audio-reactive strobe effect. On the APC40, each column of buttons triggers a clip and one knob controls the blur. At the drop, he raises the opacity of the particle layer and triggers the strobe — all with his hands, without touching the mouse.

Summary

  • Resolume = Composition → Layers → Clips; the top layer mixes by blend and opacity.
  • Decks organize the set; BPM and Audio FX sync the visuals with the music.
  • Advanced Output maps the output to the big screen and MIDI lets you play with your hands.

Layers, Clips and Composition in Resolume Arena

Resolume Arena organizes visual content into stacked layers: each layer plays an independent clip and can have its own opacity, blend mode and effects. The final result is the composition of all active layers, mixed according to the chosen mode — Add sums luminosity (ideal for lights), Screen avoids excessive saturation, Multiply darkens where there is overlap. The VJ triggers and swaps clips in real time, adjusts parameters on the beat and uses the crossfader to transition between groups of layers, creating a visual narrative synced with the music.

9f3894e5-b753-4183-a302-c5374c579115.titulo: Media servers and professional playback: Watchout, Modulo Pi and timecode
9f3894e5-b753-4183-a302-c5374c579115.conteudo_rico:
To begin: When content has to run with millisecond precision and across multiple screens, the media server comes in. It's the high-reliability playback of the big stages.

For big shows and scenography, the improvised VJ gives way to the media server: software/hardware dedicated to playing pre-produced content with perfect sync across several outputs, controlled by timecode. This lesson introduces the main systems and the concept of synchronization by SMPTE/timecode.

  • What a media server is: A system dedicated to playing and distributing content (videos, graphics, layers) across multiple screens/projectors with very high reliability and sync, usually with robust hardware and several outputs.
  • Watchout (Dataton): The standard for shows and exhibitions: a precise timeline, support for multiple screens with soft-edge blending, controlled by timecode. Excellent for timed sequences.
  • Modulo Pi (Modulo Player/Kinetic): A French media server widely used in mapping and large scenography, with 3D mapping, blending and management of many outputs.
  • disguise (d3) and Pixera: disguise is a reference in tours and TV, with 3D previsualization of the stage (previs) and pixel-canvas management; Pixera (AV Stumpfl) is another strong platform for mapping and multiscreen.
  • Millumin and PlaybackPro: Millumin is intuitive for theater/installations; PlaybackPro Plus is a simple, reliable playback of clips in order (cue by cue), widely used in corporate to 'roll the next video' with no error.
  • Timecode and SMPTE: Timecode (format HH:MM:SS:FF) is a 'clock' that synchronizes video, audio and light. SMPTE/MTC/LTC lets the media server follow exactly the tempo of the music/show, triggering content on the right frame.
  • Pixel canvas and output mapping: The server has a large virtual 'canvas' where you position the content and crop the regions that go to each screen/projector, blending the seams.
  • Redundancy (backup): In big productions a second mirrored server is used that takes over if the main one fails (failover), ensuring the show doesn't stop.

Equipment / details

  • Watchout (Dataton) + display nodes: A production server plus nodes that feed each screen, synchronized by network and timecode.
  • Modulo Pi (Modulo Player): A media server for large mapping/scenography with 3D, blending and many outputs.
  • disguise (gx/vx) / Pixera: Tour and broadcast platforms with 3D previs and pixel-canvas management.
  • Timecode generator/reader (LTC/MTC): Provides the SMPTE clock that synchronizes video, audio and light in the show.
  • Redundant server (backup): A second mirrored machine for automatic failover in critical productions.

Step by step

  1. 1. Build the pixel canvas: Define the total resolution of the content and position each element on the virtual canvas.
  2. 2. Map the outputs to the screens: Crop the regions of the canvas that feed each screen/projector; blend the seams.
  3. 3. Build the timeline with cues: Place videos/graphics on the timeline at the exact times of the show (opening, song 1, etc.).
  4. 4. Link to timecode: Set the server to follow the show's LTC/MTC, so the content triggers on the right frame with the music.
  5. 5. Configure redundancy: Mirror the content on a second server and test the failover.
  6. 6. Rehearse with the band/operation: Run the timeline with the real rehearsal timecode to check the sync of video, audio and light.

In practice: At a show with an LED set and timecode, each song's content is on a Watchout timeline. When the band plays, the audio sends LTC to Watchout, which automatically triggers the videos on the exact frame across all screens, with perfect blend between the panels. A second mirrored Watchout is on standby — if the main one goes down, the backup takes over without the audience noticing.

Summary

  • A media server delivers reliable, synchronized playback across multiple screens.
  • Timecode (SMPTE/LTC/MTC) is the clock that synchronizes video, audio and light.
  • Pixel canvas, blending and redundancy are the pillars of big productions.

Media Servers and Professional Playback

Media servers like Watchout and Modulo Pi are computers dedicated to synchronized video playback across multiple outputs. Each output can feed projectors, LED panels or monitors with distinct resolutions and formats. Timecode (TC) is the protocol that ensures sync between the video server, the sound console, the lighting and other equipment — it can be generated internally or received via LTC (audio), MTC (MIDI) or network protocols like ArtNet. Layers allow composing content with control of opacity and blend modes, and the cue list defines the marking points of the event's script.

3fcd525f-7bb3-4a36-b52c-3806a5702d90.titulo: Video mapping: warp, mask, blending and the step by step
3fcd525f-7bb3-4a36-b52c-3806a5702d90.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Mapping is turning any object into a screen. The magic comes from the precise alignment between the content and the real geometry of the surface.

Video mapping (mapped projection) projects an image onto 3D or irregular surfaces — buildings, sets, objects — making the content 'dress' the shape. This lesson explains the concepts (warp, mask, mesh, blend) and gives a step by step of a simple mapping using MadMapper/Resolume.

  • The mapping concept: Instead of a flat rectangular screen, you project onto a real shape and deform the content so each part falls exactly onto the corresponding surface.
  • Warp (deformation): Adjusts the corners and the mesh of the image to fit the perspective of the surface (keystone and distortion correction). You drag points until the image 'sticks' to the object.
  • Mask: Crops the projection so the light only appears where it should (on the facade, on the object) and doesn't spill onto the surroundings. It defines the exact outline of the projected area.
  • Slices and mesh: The composition is cut into slices, each mapped to a part of the surface. Mesh warping uses a flexible grid for curved/irregular surfaces.
  • Blending (soft-edge): When several projectors cover a large surface, the edges overlap and the software smooths the seam (blend) to look like a single image, with no brighter line.
  • Content made for the shape: The content must be produced considering the geometry: animated elements that respect edges, windows, columns. A model/template of the surface guides the designer.
  • Alignment and fine-tuning: A grid is projected onto the object and warp/mask are adjusted until each line falls on the right edge; small shifts break the illusion.

Equipment / details

  • MadMapper (software): Specialized in mapping: warp, mask, mesh, slices and multi-projector output with blend. An interface focused on surfaces.
  • Resolume Arena (Advanced Output): Does slicing, warp, mask and soft-edge in the Output, uniting VJing and mapping in a single software.
  • Laser projector with lens shift: Lets you position the projector and align the image to the object without excessive keystone.
  • HeavyM / Millumin: Mapping alternatives; HeavyM is simple for beginners, Millumin is strong in theater/installations.

Step by step

  1. 1. Model the surface: Take photos/measurements of the object and create a template with its shapes so the designer produces the right content.
  2. 2. Position the projector: Place the projector covering the whole surface, using lens shift to minimize keystone.
  3. 3. Project an alignment grid: Send a grid test pattern onto the object to see the real deformation.
  4. 4. Create slices and warp: In the software, slice the composition and drag the corners of each slice until they align with the edges of the object.
  5. 5. Apply masks: Crop the projection so the light only hits the desired surface, with no spill.
  6. 6. Do blending if multi-projector: Overlap the projectors' edges and adjust the soft-edge until the seam disappears.
  7. 7. Run the content and fine-tune: Play the real content and fix small misalignments until the illusion is perfect.

In practice: To map a facade with three windows, the technician measures the facade, creates a template and asks the designer for animations that 'light up' the windows. In MadMapper, he slices the image into slices (one per window + the wall), warps each slice until it matches the real edges and masks the surroundings so the light doesn't spill onto the sidewalk. An alignment grid ensures each animation falls exactly on the right window.

Summary

  • Mapping deforms the content (warp) and crops it (mask) to dress a real surface.
  • Slices, mesh and blending make it possible to cover complex, large shapes with several projectors.
  • The content must be made for the geometry, with fine alignment guided by a grid.

Video Mapping: Warp, Mask and Edge Blending

Video mapping is the technique of projecting visual content onto unconventional surfaces — facades, sets, 3D objects — with geometric precision. Warp corrects distortions caused by the projector's angle or position, deforming the output digitally so the content fits the surface perfectly. The mask crops the visible area of the projection, excluding unwanted regions such as windows or frames. When two or more projectors cover the same screen, edge blending progressively attenuates the overlapping edges to eliminate the visible seam. Mastering these three processes and executing them in the correct sequence is what separates an amateur projection from a professional production.

fb55c65c-c367-48c1-8961-74cd0f6c8062.titulo: Editing for events: cutting, transitions, tracks, channels and export
fb55c65c-c367-48c1-8961-74cd0f6c8062.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Even live, a lot of content is pre-produced. Knowing how to prepare and export media in the right format avoids freezes on stage.

The video technician also prepares the content: openers, idents, loops, backgrounds. This lesson covers the editing fundamentals relevant to events — timeline, cut, transition, effects, tracks/channels — and, crucially, how to export (encode) in the right format and codec to run smoothly in Resolume/media server and on the big screens.

  • Timeline, tracks and channels: Editing happens on a timeline with stacked tracks: video over video, audio in separate channels. Upper tracks cover the lower ones; audio channels (L/R, stereo) follow the video.
  • Cut: Split and remove segments to build the sequence. Precise cuts define the rhythm of an ident or opener.
  • Transitions: Fade, dissolve, wipe between clips. In event content, clean transitions on the beat of the music work better than an excess of effects.
  • Effects and color correction: Color grading makes the content cohesive and suitable for the big screen (LED tends to blow out — you control brightness/contrast). Effects should serve the message, not compete with it.
  • Alpha channel and transparency: For logos/elements that come on top (in the DSK/layer), you export with an alpha channel (e.g., ProRes 4444, PNG sequence) to preserve transparency.
  • Seamless loops: Stage backgrounds need to repeat without a 'jump'. You edit the first and last frame to match, creating a continuous loop.
  • Codec and container for playback: For VJ/media server, prefer codecs that are light on CPU/GPU and high quality: HAP (optimized for GPU, ideal in Resolume), DXV (Resolume's native codec), ProRes (high quality, heavier). H.264/H.265 is great for distribution, but can be heavy for many live layers.
  • Export resolution and aspect ratio: Export at the exact resolution of the destination (big screen) and in the right aspect ratio; never let the player rescale on stage.

Equipment / details

  • Editing software (Premiere, DaVinci Resolve, After Effects): Premiere/Resolve for cutting and color; After Effects for motion graphics, loops and elements with alpha.
  • HAP / DXV codec: Codecs optimized for live playback in Resolume; they decompress on the GPU, light for many layers.
  • ProRes 4444: High quality with an alpha channel, great for masters and elements with transparency.
  • HAP encode plugin: Lets you export HAP directly from the editing software for use in Resolume.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the destination and specs: Know the resolution, aspect ratio and frame rate of the big screen before you start editing.
  2. 2. Edit on the timeline: Build cuts and transitions on the beat; apply color correction thinking about the LED's brightness.
  3. 3. Handle alpha when needed: For overlaid elements, work and export with an alpha channel.
  4. 4. Create seamless loops: For backgrounds, make the start and end match to repeat without a jump.
  5. 5. Export in the right codec: For Resolume, export in HAP/DXV; for masters/alpha, ProRes 4444; at the exact resolution of the destination.
  6. 6. Test in the playback software: Import the file into Resolume/media server and confirm FPS, looping and color before the event.

In practice: For a stage background on 1536x768 LED at 60p, the editor builds a particle loop in After Effects, adjusts the first/last frame to match and exports in HAP at the exact resolution. In Resolume, the clip loops smoothly, light on the GPU, with no rescaling. The ident with the event logo, meanwhile, comes out in ProRes 4444 with alpha, to come on top of the cameras in the switcher's DSK with no background.

Summary

  • Editing for events prioritizes clean cuts, color suited to the LED and seamless loops.
  • Use an alpha channel for overlaid elements and the exact resolution of the destination.
  • Codec matters: HAP/DXV for light playback in Resolume; ProRes for masters with alpha.

Video Editing for Live Events

In editing for events, the timeline is organized into tracks: V1 for the main video, GFX for graphics and captions, A1/A2 for distinct audio channels (stage, ambience, musical BG). The straight cut is the default transition because it conveys energy and agility; transitions like Dissolve and Dip to Black are used at rhythm changes or time passages. The audio channels have independent faders that set the balance between stage microphones, ambient sound and background music, all routed to a master bus. The final export requires choosing a format (MP4/MOV/MXF), resolution, frame rate (29.97 fps for NTSC broadcast, 25 fps for PAL/European streaming) and bitrate — the higher the bitrate, the higher the quality and the larger the file.

1659c14b-12d7-40e3-aede-ec54126a8bea.titulo: Advanced concepts: working resolution, encode, format and media management
1659c14b-12d7-40e3-aede-ec54126a8bea.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Organization and technical standardization are what separate the amateur from the professional. Here you consolidate resolution, encode and the event's media management.

This lesson ties the technical concepts to the practice of organization: how to standardize the resolution and encode of the entire project, choose formats, and manage media and storage so nothing is lost or freezes on the day of the event.

  • Project working resolution: Standardize a master resolution consistent with the destinations (big screen/projection). Produce and export everything in it to avoid chained rescaling.
  • Encode and bitrate: Encode is how the video is compressed. High bitrate = more quality and weight; low = light but with artifacts. For live playback, prefer intra-frame codecs (HAP/ProRes) over heavily compressed inter-frame (heavy H.264), which demands more from the CPU.
  • Format and container: The container (MP4, MOV) wraps video+audio; the codec is the internal compression. Knowing the right pairing (e.g., MOV+ProRes, MOV/AVI+HAP) ensures compatibility with the playback software.
  • Pixels, color depth and gamut: Beyond the count, the bit depth (8/10 bits) and color space matter. 10 bits avoids banding in gradients; the big screen/processor must support it.
  • Media management and naming: Name files clearly (event_scene_version_resolution) and version them (v1, v2, final). Avoid 'final_final2' — use a convention and dates.
  • Storage and backup: SSD/NVMe for active media, HDD/network for archive. Have at least two copies (3-2-1: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site). Carry the event's media on more than one flash drive/SSD.
  • Folder organization: Standard structure: /Content, /Masters, /Export_BigScreen, /Backup. Clear folders speed up the assembly and avoid running the wrong file live.

Equipment / details

  • External SSD/NVMe (e.g., Samsung T7/T9, NVMe in a case): The event's active media with fast read; carry a duplicate as backup.
  • NAS / network drive: Central archive and backup of the project, accessible by the team.
  • Transcode software (Media Encoder, Shutter Encoder): Converts/standardizes files to the event's codec/resolution (e.g., generate HAP of everything).

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the project's technical standard: Fix the master resolution, frame rate, codec and container for all content.
  2. 2. Transcode everything to the standard: Convert the received files to the event's codec/resolution, eliminating surprises on stage.
  3. 3. Standardize the naming: Apply a clear naming and versioning convention to all files.
  4. 4. Structure the folders: Organize into folders for content, masters, export and backup.
  5. 5. Do a 3-2-1 backup: Keep 3 copies on 2 media with 1 off-site; carry the event's media duplicated.
  6. 6. Validate in playback: Open the final files in the event's software and confirm they run smoothly.

In practice: Before a show, the technician receives videos in varied formats (MP4 H.264, MOV ProRes, some 30p, others 24p). He transcodes everything to HAP 1536x768 at 60p in Shutter Encoder, names it as 'show_song03_v2_1536x768.mov', organizes it into /Export_BigScreen, and carries it on two identical SSDs. On stage, everything runs smoothly in Resolume because it's standardized — no rescaling or heavy codec freezing.

Summary

  • Standardize resolution, frame rate, codec and container across the whole project.
  • For live playback, prefer light intra-frame codecs (HAP/ProRes) over heavy H.264.
  • Naming, folders and a 3-2-1 backup avoid running the wrong file or losing media.

Working Resolution, Encode, Format and Media Management

The working resolution defines the visual quality and the data volume throughout the production pipeline: the higher the resolution and color depth, the higher the bitrate and disk space needed. The chosen codec (H.264, ProRes, DNxHD, RAW) determines the balance between quality, compression, compatibility and processing load — each stage of the pipeline requires the right format to avoid quality loss or bottlenecks. Media management organizes the files into clear categories (master, proxy, delivery, backup, temporary) to ensure traceability and safety of the content. On LED panels, the pixel pitch and the viewer's distance dictate the perceived resolution: the rule of thumb is that the minimum distance in meters equals the pitch in millimeters multiplied by 0.7.

6ebdfbb2-c6b9-4bd9-b486-03f040b2aa0e.titulo: Briefing, technical rider, documentation and industry jargon
6ebdfbb2-c6b9-4bd9-b486-03f040b2aa0e.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The best technician in the world fails if they don't understand what the client wants and don't document the operation. Communication and paperwork are part of the craft.

This lesson closes the course with the project and communication side: how to read/create a briefing, put together a technical rider, document the event, organize files and crew, and master the jargon to talk as an equal with production, audio, lighting and stage.

  • Briefing: Initial document/conversation that defines the objective, audience, message, visual references and client expectations. It's the basis of everything: without understanding the briefing, you deliver something technically correct but wrong for the event.
  • Script and rundown: Minute-by-minute sequence of the event: what happens, when, which content comes in, who triggers it. The rundown aligns video with audio, lighting and stage.
  • Technical requirements and technical rider: The rider is the list of what you need: equipment, power, cabling, operating position, screen resolution, input/output signals, internet point. Deliver it to production in advance.
  • Signal map and cabling diagram: Document the signal chain (what goes where, which converters, which outputs). This diagram speeds up setup and troubleshooting on the day.
  • Crew and roles: Define who does what: switcher operator, VJ, LED, cabling. At larger events, there's hierarchy and communication over intercom.
  • Documentation and file organization: Keep briefing, rider, diagrams, content and versions in an organized repository (cloud/standardized folder), accessible to the crew and archived after the event.
  • Communication at the event (intercom): Headsets/intercom connect video, audio, lighting and directing. Use clear, objective language ('stand by camera 2', 'roll VT', 'cut').
  • Technical jargon: Master the vocabulary: PGM/PVW, VT (videotape, the video to roll), 'stand by', 'on air', tally, DSK, cue, blackout, feed, return, GFX, lower-third, timecode, blend, cabinet, pitch, nits, throw ratio.
  • Licenses and rights: Third-party content (music, videos, fonts) may require a license. Check the rights of the material being projected to avoid legal problems.

Equipment / details

  • Intercom system (e.g., Clear-Com, Hollyland): Headset communication between video, audio, lighting and directing during the event.
  • Diagram software (e.g., draw.io, Visio): To draw the signal map and rider clearly for production.
  • Cloud/repository (Drive, Dropbox): Centralizes briefing, rider, diagrams and content, with versions, accessible to the crew.

Step by step

  1. 1. Gather the briefing: Understand objective, audience, message and references; clear up doubts before planning.
  2. 2. Build the rundown: List the event minute by minute, marking content cues and who triggers them.
  3. 3. Write the technical rider: List equipment, power, cabling, operating position and screen resolution; send it to production.
  4. 4. Draw the signal map: Document the full chain, from source to destination, with converters and outputs.
  5. 5. Define the crew and communication: Assign roles and establish intercom and standard language.
  6. 6. Organize and archive the documentation: Centralize everything in a standardized folder/cloud; archive after the event for reuse.

In practice: For a corporate event, the technician receives the briefing (talk + 21:9 screen + broadcast), builds the rundown with production marking when each slide and VT comes in, writes the rider (switcher, 2 cameras, processor, power, internet point, operating desk at the back of the room) and draws the signal map. On the day, with everything documented and the intercom on, setup flows and any problem is diagnosed from the diagram in minutes.

Summary

  • Briefing and rundown ensure you deliver what the event actually needs.
  • The technical rider and signal map align production and speed up setup/troubleshooting.
  • Clear communication (intercom/jargon), organized documentation and licenses complete the professionalism.

Briefing, Technical Rider and Documentation

The technical rider is the document issued by the video service provider specifying all the equipment, connections, electrical circuits and conditions needed to carry out the work — its absence or delay compromises the venue's infrastructure and can make the event unfeasible. The briefing, in turn, is the starting point provided by the client: it defines objective, audience, location and budget, being the basis of any consistent technical proposal. Mastering the documentation of the full cycle — from briefing to debriefing, including budget, ART, floor plan and pre-show checklist — is what separates the market professional from the improvised operator.

8cd7ef59-d8dc-41c9-8c4e-2dc90d04f317.titulo: Switchers (Video Mixers): ATEM, Roland and Analog Way
8cd7ef59-d8dc-41c9-8c4e-2dc90d04f317.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Every broadcast and every live event with more than one image source goes through a switcher. It's the heart of video operation: it's where you choose, mix and deliver the final image.

In this lesson you'll understand what a switcher (video mixer) is, the PGM/PVW logic, the concept of the keyer (chroma and luma), the types of transition and how to size inputs/outputs. We'll compare the most-used real models on the market, from entry level (Blackmagic ATEM Mini) to professional broadcast (Analog Way, Roland V-800HD).

  • What is a switcher: A switcher is the equipment that receives multiple video sources (cameras, computer, players) and lets you select which one goes on air, apply transitions, overlay graphics and generate a single program output (PGM). It's the difference between 'recording a camera' and 'directing a live program'.
  • PGM and PVW (Program and Preview): PGM (Program) is what's ON AIR now; it's the signal going to recording, streaming and the screen. PVW (Preview, also called Preset) is the next source you're preparing to bring in — you select it in PVW and, when you trigger the transition (CUT/AUTO), it swaps places with PGM. Working with PVW prevents cutting to a wrong or badly framed source.
  • Buses, M/E and mixing: Each bus is a row of source-selection buttons. Professional mixers have one or more M/E (Mix/Effects) blocks: each M/E has its own PGM/PVW and keyers, and the result of one M/E can become a source of another — this allows layered compositions. Entry-level mixers typically have 1 M/E.
  • Keyers: chroma key and luma key: The keyer cuts out part of an image to overlay it over another. Chroma key cuts out a color (green/blue background) — used for a presenter over a virtual set. Luma key cuts out by brightness (black/white) — used for lower thirds and graphics. DSK (Downstream Keyer) is a final layer applied over everything, ideal for a permanent logo or credits.
  • Transitions: CUT is the hard cut (instant swap). MIX/DISSOLVE is the crossfade. WIPE is a transition with a shape (curtain/diagonal). STING/DVE are animated transitions with video or a digital effect. The choice sets the pace: cut for dynamism, dissolve for mood.
  • Inputs, outputs and formats: Count the inputs by the number of simultaneous sources (4, 8, 12, 20...). Check whether they are HDMI (consumer/AV) or SDI (broadcast, long cable). Important outputs: PGM (program), AUX (auxiliary/independent output, used for the screen or ISO), Multiview (all sources on one screen) and recording/streaming.

Equipment / details

  • Blackmagic ATEM Mini / Mini Pro / Mini Extreme ISO: Compact USB entry-level switcher. Mini: 4 HDMI inputs, output via USB (the camera appears as a webcam on the PC). Mini Pro: adds direct streaming over Ethernet and USB recording. Mini Extreme ISO: 8 HDMI inputs, multiview, 2 keyers, and records each input isolated (ISO) + a DaVinci Resolve project. When to use: lives, video podcasts, worship services and small/medium events with HDMI sources. Limit: HDMI has short range, no large physical panel on the Mini model.
  • Blackmagic ATEM Constellation / ATEM 2 M/E / 4 M/E: Broadcast rack line with SDI inputs (8 to 40), multiple M/E, several keyers per M/E and several AUX outputs. Controlled by software (ATEM Software Control) or by a physical Hardware Panel. When to use: TV, large events, SDI multicamera with long cabling.
  • Roland V-60HD: HD video mixer with a dedicated physical panel: 6 inputs (4 SDI + 2 HDMI, configurable), real PGM/PVW, 1 PinP + 1 key, transitions and embedded audio with a mixer. When to use: live operation with physical buttons, churches, auditoriums and event productions that want tactile hardware without relying on a laptop.
  • Roland V-800HD MK II: Roland's top presentation/event switcher: 8 inputs with independent scalers on each (accepts different resolutions and formats), 2 M/E, luma/chroma keyers, multiformat output. When to use: corporate events with mixed sources (camera + laptop at varying resolutions), shows and large screens.
  • Roland VR-series (VR-4HD / VR-50HD MK II / VR-120HD): AV switchers with an integrated audio mixer and USB output for streaming. VR-120HD: up to 12 inputs (HDMI/SDI), scaler on all, touch multiview. When to use: 'all-in-one' production (video + audio + streaming) for webinars, churches and hybrid rooms.
  • Analog Way Aquilon RS / Picturall / LiveCore Eikos: Extremely high-end presentation switchers/processors for large screens and blends. Multiple layers, mixing of 4K/8K sources, soft-edge blending between projectors, screen mapping. When to use: large conferences, stages with huge screens and multiple surfaces. It's the rental/pro-AV standard.
  • NewTek / Vizrt TriCaster (TC1, Mini): Software switcher on dedicated hardware, strong in NDI: dozens of inputs over the network, ISO recording, replay, graphics, virtual sets and integrated streaming. When to use: studios producing with IP/NDI sources that want replay, graphics and multicamera in a single system.

Step by step

  1. 1. Map the sources: List all sources (cameras, laptop, player) and their formats (HDMI/SDI, 1080p60, etc.). The switcher needs one input per simultaneous source.
  2. 2. Define the project format: On the switcher, set the output format (e.g., 1080p60). Sources with a different format go through the scaler (if any) or need to be converted first.
