  3. 3. Connect and identify inputs: Plug each source into the corresponding input and name it in the multiview. Check sync: sources without sync can 'flash' on the cut (on mixers without per-input frame sync).
  4. 4. Work with PVW: Always select the next source in PVW/Preset, check the framing and only then trigger AUTO or CUT to put it in PGM.
  5. 5. Configure keyers: Put the logo on the DSK (upstream/downstream). For chroma, adjust the key color, spill and softness over the green background.
  6. 6. Define outputs: Route PGM to recording/streaming and AUX to the screen. Check on the multiview before opening the event.

In practice: In a worship service with 3 SDI cameras and a laptop with lyrics/slides, a Roland V-60HD handles it easily: 4 SDI + 2 HDMI, physical PGM/PVW and an audio mixer. You keep the wide camera in PVW, prepare the close-up in PVW before each swap and use the DSK for the church logo fixed in the corner. Meanwhile, in a home webinar with 2 HDMI cameras and a shared screen, an ATEM Mini Pro delivers everything: cutting, direct streaming and recording to a USB drive.

Summary

  • The switcher selects, mixes and delivers the final image: PGM is what's on air, PVW is what you prepare beforehand.
  • Keyers (chroma/luma/DSK) overlay graphics and cutouts; transitions (cut, mix, wipe) set the pace.
  • Choose by the number and type of inputs: ATEM Mini for HDMI/lives, Roland V-60/V-800 for tactile hardware, Analog Way/TriCaster for large events.

Video Switchers: Program, Preview and Transitions

A video switcher (video mixer) is the heart of any live production: it decides which signal goes on air (Program bus) and which will be the next source (Preview bus). Equipment like the BlackMagic ATEM, Roland V-8HD and Analog Way Pulse² all follow this same two-bus logic. The transition between sources can be a hard cut (CUT), a dissolve (MIX), a dip to black (DIP) or a wipe (WIPE), with a duration adjustable by the operator via T-Bar or the AUTO button. The DSK (Downstream Keyer) lets you overlay graphics — such as captions and logos — independently of the main video flow.

13e8692d-5ad5-4b97-9235-59d47d847911.titulo: Media Servers and Software: Resolume, Watchout, disguise and Green Hippo
13e8692d-5ad5-4b97-9235-59d47d847911.conteudo_rico:
To get started: When the content stops being 'a live camera' and becomes video, graphics and mapping over sets, stages and irregular screens, the media server steps in. It plays, organizes and distributes the show's visual content.

In this lesson you'll understand what a media server is, the logic of layers and compositions, what projection mapping / output mapping is, and how these systems distribute the signal to several projectors and panels. We compare Resolume Arena, Dataton Watchout, disguise (d3) and Green Hippo Hippotizer.

  • What is a media server: It's a system (software + powerful hardware, usually with several video outputs) that plays clips, graphics and real-time generated content and distributes it in a synchronized way to projectors and screens. It's what turns a video file into a show mapped onto a 3D set, an LED floor or a blend of projectors.
  • Layers and composition: As in an editor, content is built up in stacked layers: background video, graphics, masks, effects. Each layer has opacity, blend mode, position and effects. You trigger/activate layers live (e.g., Resolume) or run on a programmed timeline (e.g., Watchout).
  • Timeline vs. live trigger: There are two philosophies: timeline (everything programmed on a timeline, ideal for scripted shows synced with audio/lighting — Watchout, disguise) and live triggering (you fire clips/effects on the spot, like a VJ — Resolume). Many systems do both.
  • Mapping: output mapping and projection mapping: Output mapping is telling the server which fraction of which output each part of the composition goes to (screen cropping, split screen). Projection mapping is warping the image so it fits a real 3D surface (building, object, set) — it uses meshes/warp and masks to align pixels with the physical geometry.
  • Soft-edge blending: When two or more projectors overlap to form a single, giant image, the server creates a blend band between them, with a gradient, so the seam doesn't show. It's a central function in large-scale projection.
  • Sync and redundancy: In large shows, several servers work together (master/backup) synced by timecode. Systems like disguise and Watchout offer failover (a spare server takes over if the main one goes down), essential in events where there can be no black screen.

Equipment / details

  • Resolume Arena / Avenue: VJ and media server software. Avenue is the live-trigger version of clips in a grid (decks/layers). Arena adds output to multiple screens, projection mapping (Advanced Output), soft-edge blending, SMPTE timecode and DMX/Art-Net support. When to use: shows, clubs, music stages, LED panels with content triggered live. Runs on a powerful PC/Mac. Strong ecosystem of effects and plugins (FFGL).
  • Dataton Watchout: Timeline-based multiscreen display system, standard in museums, fixed installations and scripted shows. Architecture: a production computer controls several display computers (each with its outputs), all synced over the network. Strong in soft-edge blending and very high-resolution screens built from a mosaic. When to use: permanent sets, exhibitions, large panels and precisely programmed frame-accurate projections.
  • disguise (formerly d3) — gx/vx/Pro Range + Designer: Cutting-edge platform for shows, tours, broadcast and xR (extended reality). The Designer software works with a 3D virtual stage: you model the set/LED in software and the content is mapped over the model. Strong in xR/AR (studios with LED volumes), camera tracking and virtual production workflows. Dedicated hardware (gx 2c/3, vx 4) with many outputs. When to use: large tours, virtual production studios, premium broadcast.
  • Green Hippo Hippotizer (Karst, Boreal, Taiga, Montane): British media server line (tvONE) widely used in rental and broadcast. Hippotizer software with a pixel-mapping engine, projection mapping (Mapper component), notch (real-time effects) and ZooKeeper for multi-user control. Models scale by number/resolution of outputs (Karst entry → Taiga top 4K/8K). When to use: corporate events, shows and TV needing many reliable outputs.
  • Avolites Ai / AiM: Media server often integrated with Avolites lighting consoles, with a 3D stage (Stage Construction), saved via 'Performance'. Strong in LED pixel mapping and light+video integration. When to use: productions where the lighting operator also triggers video, shows and festivals.
  • PVP / QLab / Millumin / MadMapper: More affordable software solutions. QLab (Mac): media/audio triggering in theater, cue-based. Millumin and MadMapper: projection mapping and LED mapping on Mac, great for artists, installations and smaller events. When to use: theater, art installations, small and medium events on a tight budget.

Step by step

  1. 1. Model the physical output: Define how many projectors/screens and the total resolution. On the server, configure the outputs and the screen/canvas matching the real physical area.
  2. 2. Build the composition in layers: Import the clips/graphics and organize them in layers: background, content, masks, effects. Adjust the opacity and blend of each one.
  3. 3. Do the output/projection mapping: In the output module (Advanced Output in Resolume, Mapper in Hippo), crop and warp the image to fit each surface/screen. Use a grid/mesh to deform it.
  4. 4. Adjust the soft-edge blending: Where two projectors overlap, set the blend band (width + gamma curve) until the seam disappears.
  5. 5. Program a timeline or cues: For a scripted show, build the timeline with timecode; for live triggering, organize clips into decks/columns to fire on the spot.
  6. 6. Set up redundancy and test: In critical events, set a backup server and timecode sync. Run a full rehearsal checking sync across all outputs.

In practice: In a music show with 3 different LED screens, Resolume Arena triggers loops and graphics live: each screen receives a crop of the canvas via Advanced Output, and the VJ activates columns in sync with the music. Meanwhile, in a museum exhibition with 6 projectors forming a continuous 12-meter wall, Dataton Watchout is the choice: precise timeline, soft-edge blending between the 6 projectors and synced display computers running 8 hours a day without intervention.

Summary

  • A media server plays and distributes synchronized visual content to several screens/projectors, built up in layers.
  • Output mapping crops/positions the image on the outputs; projection mapping warps the image for real 3D surfaces; soft-edge merges overlapping projectors.
  • Resolume is live triggering (VJ); Watchout is timeline for installations; disguise leads xR/tours; Green Hippo/Avolites Ai serve rental and LED.

Media Servers: the heart of live video

A media server is the software that receives, processes and delivers video content in real time to projectors, LED panels and other outputs. It operates with stacked layers, each with independent opacity and blend mode — a concept analogous to Photoshop, but at 60 fps. Resolume Arena is preferred in smaller events and VJ work for its intuitive interface and strong MIDI/DMX support; Watchout and disguise dominate large productions and XR thanks to their ability to map complex surfaces and synchronize multiple servers; the Hippotizer (Green Hippo) balances flexibility and lighting integration via CITP. Choosing the right software depends on the scale of the event, the type of projection surface and the available budget.

d7bafede-fd0f-44fe-be6f-c0bcbcc89874.titulo: LED Panels and Processors: Pixel Pitch, Cabinets and NovaStar
d7bafede-fd0f-44fe-be6f-c0bcbcc89874.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The LED panel has become the standard screen for stages, churches, studios and booths. But between buying/renting well and spending badly lies understanding pixel pitch, cabinets and the processor that commands everything.

In this lesson you'll learn how an LED panel is built (modules, cabinets), what pixel pitch is and how it defines viewing distance and resolution, the role of the receiving card and the processor/sending card (NovaStar, Colorlight), plus the most relevant panel brands (Absen, Unilumin, ROE, INFiLED).

  • Anatomy of the panel: from LED to screen: The smallest unit is the pixel (a set of R, G, B LEDs). Several pixels form a module. Modules fit into a cabinet — the piece you stack. Several locked cabinets form the screen. Each cabinet has a receiving card behind it that receives the data and powers the LEDs.
  • Pixel pitch (P2, P3, P4...): Pixel pitch is the distance in millimeters between the centers of two neighboring pixels. P2 = 2 mm, P3 = 3 mm, P4 = 4 mm. The SMALLER the pitch, the more pixels per meter, the higher the resolution and the closer the audience can be without seeing the 'dots'. The LARGER the pitch, the less resolution, the cheaper it is, and the farther away the audience needs to be. Close-up indoor stage: P2.6–P3.9. Large, distant outdoor: P6–P10.
  • Minimum viewing distance: Rule of thumb: the ideal minimum distance in meters is roughly equal to the pixel pitch in mm (the 1 m per 1 mm rule). A P3 looks good from ~3 m; a P10, from ~10 m. Below that the image 'pixelates'. Use this to choose the pitch based on where the audience will be.
  • Total resolution and cabinet count: The screen's resolution is the sum of the cabinets' pixels. E.g.: a 168×168 px cabinet; a screen of 6×3 cabinets = 1008×504 px. You need to know the total resolution to configure the media server/switcher output and for the processor to map each cabinet.
  • Brightness (nits), refresh rate and color: Brightness in nits/cd/m²: indoor ~600–1500 nits; outdoor 4000–6000+ nits to beat the sun. For CAMERA (studio/IMAG), what matters is a high refresh rate (≥1920 Hz, ideally 3840 Hz+) and low flicker, otherwise bands/scanlines appear in the video. Color depth and calibration affect fidelity.
  • Processor (sending card) and receiving card: The processor/sending card receives the video (HDMI/SDI/DVI), scales it, adjusts color/brightness and maps the image pixel by pixel over the cabinets, sending it via network cable to each cabinet's receiving card. It's the brain of the screen: without correct mapping on the processor, the cabinets show scrambled pieces of the image.

Equipment / details

  • NovaStar (sending: MCTRL4K, VX4S, VX1000, VX16S; receiving: A5s/A8s/A10s Plus; Coex): World leader in LED processing. Sending cards/processors: VX4S (entry, all-in-one scaler+sending), VX1000/VX16S (high capacity, 4K), MCTRL4K (pure 4K control). NovaLCT software (configuration) and management via the receiving card. When to use: practically the standard of the rental and fixed market; match the processor to the total resolution and refresh rate the camera requires.
  • NovaStar MX40 Pro / CX40 / H-series: Premium processors for broadcast and xR: extremely low latency, high refresh, advanced color management and multiple Ethernet/10G outputs. When to use: virtual studios (LED volume), broadcast and shows where camera and low latency are critical.
  • Colorlight (Z6 Pro, X16 Pro; receiving 5A/i5A; LEDVISION software): Strong Chinese competitor to NovaStar, common in cost-competitive panels. Sending cards and processors with a scaler, LEDVISION software. When to use: fixed installations and cost-optimized rental; very present in panels sold in Brazil.
  • Absen (PL/AX/Polaris/Acclaim series): High-standard panel maker (not a processor), widely used in premium rental and broadcast. Fine-pitch indoor lines (Absen Polaris, PL2.5/2.9/3.9) and outdoor. When to use: when you want a reliable panel and consistent calibration for stages and studios.
  • Unilumin (UpadIII, Uslim, Upanel, Umini/UTV): High-volume Chinese maker, with lines for rental (UpadIII), fixed and All-in-One (Umini for meeting rooms/studio). When to use: a wide range of pitch and application, from the stage to the corporate all-in-one screen.
  • ROE Visual (Black Pearl BP2/BP3, Carbon, Diamond) and INFiLED: ROE Visual is a benchmark of very high standard in rental/broadcast and xR (cinema/TV LED volumes use ROE). INFiLED is another pro stage/studio brand. When to use: top-of-the-line productions, virtual studios and tours where image uniformity and quality are non-negotiable.
  • Brompton Technology (Tessera SX40/S8) — premium processing: Premium LED processor for broadcast and virtual production, standard in xR studios alongside ROE panels. Features like Dynamic Calibration, high color depth and low latency. When to use: LED volume and broadcast where on-camera image quality is the top priority.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define where the audience will be: Measure the minimum distance from the audience to the screen. Use the ~1 m per 1 mm of pitch rule to choose P2/P3/P4, etc.
  2. 2. Size the screen: Define width×height in cabinets and calculate the total resolution (cabinet px × number of cabinets on each axis).
  3. 3. Choose the panel by application: Indoor stage: fine pitch + high refresh if there's a camera. Outdoor: high brightness (4000+ nits) and IP protection.
  4. 4. Configure the processor: On the processor (e.g., NovaStar via NovaLCT), map the cabinets (order, wiring) so the image appears correct and continuous.
  5. 5. Adjust for camera (if there's IMAG): Raise the refresh rate (3840 Hz+), adjust the camera's brightness/shutter to eliminate scanlines and calibrate the color.
  6. 6. Feed the processor with the video: Connect the switcher/media server output to the processor input, checking that the resolution/format matches the mapped canvas.

In practice: In a church with a stage and cameras for the broadcast screen, an indoor P3.9 panel with a NovaStar VX4S processor works: the closest audience is about 4 m away (fine for P3.9) and, since there's a camera, a high refresh is set so no bands appear in the live. Meanwhile, in an outdoor show with the audience at 15 m, a P6/P10 at 5000 nits is used to beat the sun — you save on pixels no one would see up close anyway, and the brightness ensures a visible image in broad daylight.

Summary

  • Pixel pitch (P2/P3/P4) is the distance between pixels in mm: smaller = more resolution and close-up viewing; larger = cheaper and long-distance viewing.
  • The screen is made of cabinets with receiving cards; the processor (sending card) maps the image pixel by pixel over them.
  • For camera, prioritize a high refresh rate; NovaStar/Colorlight process, and Absen/Unilumin/ROE/INFiLED make the panels.

LED Panels: Pixel Pitch, Cabinets and Processors

Pixel pitch defines the distance between the centers of two adjacent LEDs — the smaller the value, the higher the density and the closer the audience can be without seeing individual pixels. Cabinets are the modular units that form the screen: combined in a grid, they determine the total resolution and the physical dimensions of the display. The video processor (like the NovaStar MX/VX/V line) receives the input signal, performs pixel mapping and distributes the content through the output ports to each cabinet — respecting per-port capacity limits and allowing redundancy and external sync (Genlock) configurations essential in live productions.

200cf42a-7ac2-4138-81b0-1e97e3b5d526.titulo: Projection: Laser Projectors, Lumens and Mapping
200cf42a-7ac2-4138-81b0-1e97e3b5d526.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Where LED can't reach — huge surfaces, buildings, 3D scenery, cinema — projection comes in. And professional projection today is laser: more brightness, longer lifespan and stable color from the start to the end of the event.

In this lesson you'll understand lumens and how to size brightness by the screen and the ambient light, technologies (laser vs lamp, 1-chip vs 3-chip DLP), throw ratio and lenses, and how projectors fit into projection mapping. We compare Epson, Panasonic, Barco and Christie.

  • Lumens: what it is and how to size it: Lumens (ANSI) measure the projector's luminous flux. The more ambient light and the larger the screen, the more lumens you need. Dark room and small screen: 3,000–5,000 lm. Auditorium with some light: 7,000–12,000 lm. Large/outdoor events or mapping: 20,000 lm and much more. Stacking projectors adds up brightness.
  • Laser vs lamp: Laser projectors (solid-state light source) have replaced the UHP lamp in production: ~20,000 h of life (vs ~2,000–3,000 for a lamp), constant brightness and color, instant on, and can operate at any angle (360°) — essential for ceiling/floor mapping. Lamp still exists in entry-level models due to cost.
  • 1-chip DLP, 3-chip DLP and LCD: LCD (Epson 3LCD): good color brightness, common in corporate. 1-chip DLP: compact, good contrast, event models. 3-chip DLP (top Barco/Christie/Panasonic): maximum brightness and fidelity, the standard for large events, cinema and building mapping. The more 'pro', generally 3-chip DLP laser.
  • Throw ratio and lenses: Throw ratio = projector-to-screen distance ÷ image width. A low ratio (short distance) projects a large image from close up; a high ratio projects from far. Pro projectors have interchangeable lenses (UST/short, standard, tele/long) — you choose the lens based on the space. Lens shift moves the image without moving the projector.
  • Geometry, warping and edge blending: Professional projectors have geometric correction (keystone, mesh warp) to fit the image onto curved or angled screens, and internal edge blending to join several projectors into a single image. In mapping, this combines with the media server.
  • Useful brightness vs losses: Screens with gain, distance, lens and ambient light reduce perceived brightness. Always size with headroom: it's better to have surplus lumens (and lower the power) than a washed-out image. For outdoor building mapping, multiply the estimate because of loss on non-white surfaces.

Equipment / details

  • Epson (Pro L-Series: L1075U, L1505U, L20000U; EB-PU series): Strong in 3LCD laser, great value for brightness and color. Pro L ranges from ~7,000 to 20,000+ lumens, with interchangeable lenses, warp/blend and 360° operation. When to use: auditoriums, churches, corporate and medium-scale mapping at good cost. WUXGA/4K enhancement depending on the model.
  • Panasonic (PT-RZ series, PT-RQ/RCQ 4K, PT-MZ): SOLID SHINE laser line, very reliable in rental. PT-RZ (1-chip) for events; PT-RQ22K/RQ35K (3-chip DLP, 4K, 20,000–30,000+ lm) for large screens and mapping. Advanced geometry and robustness. When to use: corporate events, shows and mapping where 24/7 reliability is required.
  • Barco (UDX, HDX, G series, F-series; Pulse): Extremely high-end broadcast/AV standard. UDX/HDX (3-chip DLP laser) reach 30,000–40,000 lm for large events and building mapping; F-series for simulation/museum. Cutting-edge 'Pulse' image processing, warping and blending. When to use: large conferences, architectural mapping, simulation and digital cinema.
  • Christie (Boxer, M 4K25/4K+ RGB, Griffyn, Crimson, Mirage): Another pro leader. Boxer 4K30/30000+ lm and the RGB pure laser line deliver extreme brightness and wide gamut for mapping and large shows. Mirage for 3D/simulation. When to use: large-scale mapping, parks, simulators and premium productions demanding brightness and color.
  • Digital Projection (Titan, Highlite, INSIGHT): British pro maker with extremely high-brightness models (INSIGHT 4K laser, tens of thousands of lumens) and 8K. When to use: domes, planetariums, simulation and extremely high-brightness events.
  • UST / installation projectors (Epson, Sony, Optoma): Ultra Short Throw project a large image right up against the wall/screen — useful in booths, shop windows and scenery where there's no recoil space, and they avoid the presenter's shadow. When to use: activations, booths and sets with little recoil.

Step by step

  1. 1. Assess the screen and ambient light: Measure the screen (width) and the ambient light level. A dark room needs fewer lumens; a bright/outdoor environment requires many more.
  2. 2. Calculate the necessary lumens: Estimate with headroom according to screen size and light; for mapping on a non-white surface, multiply the estimate to compensate for losses.
  3. 3. Choose technology and lens: Decide laser (almost always) and 1-chip or 3-chip according to the scale. Calculate the throw ratio and select the lens (short/standard/tele) for the available distance.
  4. 4. Position and adjust geometry: Mount the projector (it can be inverted/angled if it's a 360° laser). Use lens shift, keystone and warp to fit the image onto the surface.
  5. 5. Do the blend (if several projectors): For a giant image with several projectors, configure edge blending (on the projector and/or the media server) until the seam disappears.
  6. 6. Feed it the content: Connect the switcher/media server output, check the resolution and, in mapping, align the content mesh to the real physical geometry.

In practice: In a building-facade mapping at night, with a beige surface and a 15 m wide screen, two stacked Barco UDX projectors at 30,000 lm ensure enough brightness, since the wall isn't white and there's loss. The media server (Resolume/Watchout) warps the content over the windows and frames, and edge blending joins the two projections into a single image. Meanwhile, in a church auditorium with a 5 m screen and controlled light, an Epson Pro L at 10,000 lumens laser delivers a sharp image all day with no lamp change.

Summary

  • Lumens size the brightness by the screen and ambient light; mapping and bright environments require many more lumens.
  • Laser replaced the lamp (long life, stable color, 360° operation); 3-chip DLP is the top for brightness/fidelity.
  • Throw ratio and lenses define distance/size; geometry, warp and edge blending fit the image onto real surfaces.

Laser Projectors, Lumens and Video Mapping

Laser projectors replaced mercury lamps with solid-state light sources with a 20,000 h lifespan and superior color stability. Luminosity is measured in ANSI lumens and must be sized against the area of the projected surface: the practical unit is the foot-candle (fc), where ≥ 6 fc ensures a legible image in a controlled environment and ≥ 12 fc is recommended for spaces with ambient light. The throw ratio (TR) defines the distance/width relationship — UST lenses (TR ≈ 0.38) project at very short distances, while XLong lenses (TR ≥ 4) serve deep stages. In video mapping, the video signal needs to be geometrically corrected via warp software to align to the real surface (cube, arch, facade), and blend zones eliminate seams between adjacent projectors.

da0574ff-8ddf-4059-85db-b5b005f987c7.titulo: Cameras and Capture: PTZ, Capture Cards and IMAG
da0574ff-8ddf-4059-85db-b5b005f987c7.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Live image begins with capture. From the remotely controlled PTZ camera to the card that turns the camera signal into something the computer understands, this is the entry point of your video.

In this lesson you'll get to know the types of camera for live (PTZ, camcorder, cinema/mirrorless), the concept of IMAG (screen), and how to get the camera image to the computer/switcher with capture cards (Elgato Cam Link, Magewell, Blackmagic) and output formats (HDMI/SDI/NDI).

  • IMAG: what it is: IMAG (Image Magnification) is projecting/displaying live on a screen the magnified image of what happens on stage, so the distant audience sees it up close. It requires cameras with good zoom, live operation and low latency from capture to the screen.
  • Types of camera for live: PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom): motorized camera controlled remotely, ideal for fixed positions operated by one person. Camcorder/ENG: handheld camera with long optical zoom, standard for event coverage. Cinema/Mirrorless (Sony, Canon, Blackmagic Pocket): cinematic look, require a capture card and attention to overheating/clean output. Broadcast tube (system camera): top for TV.
  • Camera outputs: HDMI, SDI, USB, IP/NDI: HDMI: common in mirrorless/consumer, short range. SDI: professional, BNC connector, long and locked cable (event/broadcast standard). USB: webcams and some cameras (UVC). IP/NDI: the camera delivers video over the network, simplifying cabling in studios. Confirm whether the camera has 'clean HDMI/SDI' (output without the on-screen menus).
  • Capture card: why it exists: The computer doesn't understand HDMI/SDI directly; the capture card converts the camera signal into something the software (OBS, Zoom, vMix) reads as a video source. It can be external (USB) or internal (PCIe). It's the link between the camera and the streaming software.
  • PTZ: control and protocols: PTZ are controlled by a joystick/controller or software via VISCA (over IP/serial), NDI or web. You create framing presets (position+zoom) and call them with a button — one person operates several cameras. Common in churches, meeting rooms and lean studios.
  • Latency, sync and genlock: In IMAG and multicamera, latency (delay) needs to be low so the screen keeps up with the stage. In broadcast, genlock syncs all cameras to the same clock for clean cuts on the switcher. USB and NDI cards introduce some latency — acceptable for live, watch out in critical IMAG.

Equipment / details

  • Elgato Cam Link 4K: Simple, popular USB capture card: takes HDMI from the camera/mirrorless and delivers it to the PC as a webcam (UVC), up to 1080p60 or 4K30. When to use: turn a mirrorless/DSLR into a live/Zoom camera directly and cheaply. Limit: 1 input, short HDMI, no loop-out.
  • Magewell (USB Capture HDMI/SDI Gen2, Pro Capture PCIe, ND/Pro Convert NDI): Reference brand in professional capture. USB Capture (plug-and-play, low latency, HDMI and SDI), Pro Capture (PCIe, several inputs for the studio), and the Pro/ND Convert line for NDI encoder/decoder. When to use: production that requires reliability and low latency (vMix, pro OBS, broadcast). More robust than consumer capture.
  • Blackmagic DeckLink / UltraStudio / Web Presenter: DeckLink: studio PCIe SDI/HDMI cards (4K, multichannel). UltraStudio: external capture/playback over USB-C/Thunderbolt. Web Presenter HD/4K: turns SDI/HDMI into a USB webcam and streams. When to use: professional SDI pipelines, integration with DaVinci Resolve and Blackmagic studios.
  • PTZ cameras (PTZOptics 30X-SDI/NDI, Sony SRG/BRC, Panasonic AW-UE series, Marshall, Avonic): PTZOptics: strong value in church/education, with SDI/HDMI/NDI output and VISCA. Sony SRG/BRC and Panasonic AW-UE (UE40/UE80/UE150): broadcast PTZ, 4K, great optics and NDI. When to use: fixed positions operated remotely, IMAG and multicamera with a small crew. Create framing presets.
  • Cinema/Mirrorless cameras (Sony A7/FX, Canon R/C, Blackmagic Pocket/URSA, Panasonic Lumix): Cinematic look for stages, shows and premium content. Blackmagic Pocket/URSA output clean SDI/HDMI; Sony FX and Canon C deliver reliable clean output. When to use: when you want depth of field and cinematic aesthetics in live. Note: check clean output, overheating and continuous power (dummy battery).
  • Camcorders / System Cameras (Sony PXW/HXC, Panasonic AG/AK, JVC): Shoulder cameras with long optical zoom and SDI, standard for event and sports coverage; system cameras (with CCU) for TV. When to use: live coverage with an operator, sports, shows — long zoom and ergonomics for hours of operation.
  • PTZ controllers (PTZOptics SuperJoy, Sony RM-IP500, Bolin) and software (vMix/OBS via VISCA): Physical controllers with a joystick for pan/tilt/zoom and preset buttons; or software control (vMix, OBS plugins) via VISCA over IP. When to use: to operate several PTZ with one person, calling presets during the live.

Step by step

  1. 1. Choose the camera by function: PTZ for fixed/remote positions, camcorder for coverage with an operator, cinema/mirrorless for aesthetics. Confirm the output (HDMI/SDI/NDI) and clean output.
  2. 2. Ensure clean output: In the camera menus, enable clean output (no icons/menus on screen) and set the format (e.g., 1080p60) the same as the switcher's.
  3. 3. Choose the capture card: One HDMI camera into the PC: Cam Link. Several inputs/low latency: Magewell or DeckLink. Long SDI: prefer SDI to the card/switcher.
  4. 4. Connect and test the source: In the software (OBS/vMix), add the card as a video source. Check resolution, color and latency. For PTZ, configure VISCA/NDI and create presets.
  5. 5. Power and secure the cables: Use a dummy battery/power supply for cameras in long live use. Lock SDI (BNC) connectors and protect HDMI against disconnection.
  6. 6. Integrate with the switcher/IMAG: Route the cameras to the switcher, and PGM to the screen (IMAG) and streaming. Check the total latency from stage to screen.

In practice: In a church with 3 PTZOptics PTZ cameras on NDI, a single person operates everything: presets for the wide shot, pulpit and audience are called from the SuperJoy controller, and vMix cuts between them for the screen (IMAG) and the live. Meanwhile, in a video podcast with two Sony mirrorless cameras, each goes out over HDMI to an Elgato Cam Link 4K; OBS receives the two as webcams, and the host switches between the shots. In both, the secret is clean output enabled and an identical 1080p60 format on all sources.

Summary

  • IMAG magnifies the stage image live on the screen; it requires zoom, low latency and good camera operation.
  • PTZ lets you operate several cameras remotely with presets; mirrorless/cinema give aesthetics but require a card and clean output.
  • The capture card (Cam Link, Magewell, Blackmagic) converts the camera's HDMI/SDI into a source for the software; mind the format and latency.

PTZ Cameras and IMAG Capture at Events

PTZ cameras (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) are controlled remotely via the VISCA, VISCA-over-IP or NDI protocol, letting the operator adjust the framing without physically touching the equipment — essential in stage environments where every cable matters. The video signal travels a well-defined chain: camera → transport interface (SDI coaxial or NDI over network) → capture card or switcher → screen/projector. In IMAG (Image Magnification) systems, the goal is to enlarge the speaker's face on the screen relative to their real size; the recommended minimum ratio is 4:1 so the distant audience feels presence. The LED panel's pixel pitch determines the minimum comfortable viewing distance (mm × 1000 ÷ 3.438), and the whole chain must operate in the same aspect (16:9) to avoid crops or distortion.

eeb3d3a7-838f-4662-abdf-e3e42083ec25.titulo: Connections and Transport: SDI, HDMI, Converters, Fiber and NDI
eeb3d3a7-838f-4662-abdf-e3e42083ec25.conteudo_rico:
To get started: It's no use having the best camera and the best screen if the signal doesn't arrive intact. Cables, connectors, converters and network protocols are the 'plumbing' the video travels through — and this is where most events break.

In this lesson you'll master the video transport media: HDMI and SDI (and their distance limits), converters (Decimator, Blackmagic Micro Converter), fiber optics (opticalCON/SDI over fiber) for long distances, and video over IP with NDI. We'll see when to use each.

  • HDMI: pros, cons and distance: HDMI carries video+audio on a consumer connector, common in cameras, laptops and screens. Weak points: the connector doesn't lock (it falls out easily), and the reliable range is short (~5–10 m on a passive cable). For longer distances you use active/optical HDMI or convert to SDI/fiber. Good for the 'last mile' (near the screen/camera).
  • SDI: the event standard: SDI (Serial Digital Interface) uses coaxial cable and a BNC connector that LOCKS. It carries uncompressed video for tens to 100+ meters (3G-SDI ~1080p60; 6G/12G-SDI for 4K). It's the broadcast and event standard for reliability and range. When there's distance and multiple cameras, think SDI.
  • Converters: the production's Swiss Army knife: Converters adapt formats and solve incompatibilities: HDMI↔SDI (cross-convert), up/down/cross-scaling (change resolution), and frame rate conversion. They're small and cheap, but they save the event when a laptop only has HDMI and the switcher only has SDI, or when a source is in a different format.
  • Fiber optics for video: For very long distances (stadiums, large events, streets), the SDI/HDMI signal is transported over fiber optics, immune to interference and capable of kilometers. Robust connectors like the Neutrik opticalCON are the stage standard. A pair of converters is used (fiber TX on one side, RX on the other).
  • NDI: video over IP: NDI (Network Device Interface, from NewTek/Vizrt) transports video, audio and control over a common Ethernet network (network cable). Advantage: many sources on a single network cable/switch, simple cabling, automatic discovery. There's full-bandwidth NDI (more quality/bandwidth) and NDI|HX (compressed, for PTZ and lower bandwidth). Note: it requires a well-sized network (gigabit+, managed switch) and introduces some latency.
  • Resolution, frame rate and color: matching formats: Most failures come from formats that don't match: resolution (1080p x 720p x 4K), frame rate (50/59.94/60) and color space/range. Standardize everything to the same format (e.g., 1080p60) from camera to screen. When you can't, use a converter/scaler to fix it before reaching the switcher.

Equipment / details

  • Blackmagic Micro Converter (HDMI↔SDI, BiDirectional, SDI Distribution): Compact, cheap converters: Micro Converter HDMI to SDI and SDI to HDMI (USB-powered), the BiDirectional version does both directions, and the SDI Distribution multiplies one SDI output into several. When to use: adapt an HDMI laptop to an SDI switcher, take SDI back to an HDMI TV, distribute a signal to several destinations. A basic item in every kit.
  • Decimator (MD-HX, MD-LX, DMON multiviewers): Reference brand in robust conversion/monitoring. MD-LX (HDMI↔SDI bidirectional), MD-HX (cross-convert with scaling/frame rate and windows), DMON (multiviewer that shows several sources on one screen). When to use: production needing reliable conversion with scaling and a portable multiview. Standard in broadcast/rental.
  • AJA (ROI, Hi5, HA5, U-TAP, FS-series): Pro maker of converters and frame syncs: Hi5 (SDI→HDMI), HA5 (HDMI→SDI), ROI (scaling with region of interest), FS-series (rack frame sync/scaler). When to use: broadcast-level conversion and sync, integrating sources in different formats with quality.
  • Blackmagic Mini Converter / Teranex / OpenGear: Line above the Micro: Mini Converters (including Optical Fiber for SDI over fiber), Teranex (very high quality conversion/processing, standards conversion), and OpenGear cards for rack. When to use: SDI over fiber, standards conversion (PAL/NTSC, frame rate) and integration into a studio rack.
  • Neutrik opticalCON (DUO/QUAD) + fiber transceivers: Robust fiber optic connector, the stage standard, with dust protection and locking. Combined with SDI-over-fiber converters (Blackmagic, AJA, Lightviper, Fiberlink), it transports video over hundreds of meters to kilometers. When to use: stadiums, large events, street broadcasts, long cable runs where coaxial can't reach.
  • NDI: NewTek/Vizrt, BirdDog (encoders/decoders), Magewell Pro Convert, Sienna/NDI Tools: Video-over-IP ecosystem. BirdDog and Magewell Pro Convert convert SDI/HDMI ↔ NDI (encoder/decoder); PTZ cameras bring native NDI; NDI Tools (Studio Monitor, Scan Converter) are free. When to use: studios and installations where cabling many sources over SDI would be unfeasible and a dedicated network is available.
  • Managed network switch (gigabit/10G) + Cat6/6A cables: Infrastructure for NDI: managed switch with IGMP snooping (for NDI multicast), gigabit or 10G ports and Cat6/6A cabling. When to use: whenever the production relies on NDI/IP video — a poorly sized network takes the video down. It's 'video equipment' just as much as the converter.

Step by step

  1. 1. Map the source and destination of each signal: List each source, its connector (HDMI/SDI) and the distance to the destination. This defines the transport medium of each run.
  2. 2. Choose the medium by distance: Short (near the screen/camera): HDMI. Medium/long and locked: SDI. Very long: fiber (opticalCON). Many sources/studio: NDI over the network.
  3. 3. Insert converters where there's incompatibility: HDMI laptop → SDI switcher: use a Micro Converter/Decimator. Different format: use a converter with a scaler before the switcher.
  4. 4. Standardize the format: Set 1080p60 (or the project's) and make every source arrive in that format; convert/scale whatever is different.
  5. 5. For fiber, set up TX/RX: SDI→fiber converter (TX) at the source, opticalCON fiber on the run, fiber→SDI converter (RX) at the destination. Test the link beforehand.
  6. 6. For NDI, prepare the network: Use a managed gigabit/10G switch with IGMP, Cat6 cables, and test bandwidth/latency. Check discovery of the NDI sources before the event.

In practice: In an event where the stage is 80 meters from the control room, HDMI is out of the question: the cameras go out over SDI (locked BNC) to the control room with no loss. When a speaker arrives with a laptop that only has HDMI, a Blackmagic Micro Converter HDMI to SDI solves it on the spot. And in a festival where the control room is 300 meters from the main stage, the signal goes over opticalCON fiber: SDI→fiber converter on stage, fiber→SDI converter in the control room. Meanwhile, in a studio with 6 PTZ, instead of pulling 6 SDI cables, everything travels over NDI on a managed gigabit switch.

Summary

  • HDMI is short and doesn't lock (last mile); SDI is the event standard, locked and long-range; fiber takes the signal hundreds of meters/kilometers.
  • Converters (Micro Converter, Decimator, AJA) solve connector and format incompatibilities — a must-have in the kit.
  • NDI transports many sources over an Ethernet network, simplifying cabling, but requires a well-sized network and tolerates some latency.

Connections and Transport: SDI, HDMI, Converters, Fiber and NDI

Each type of video connection has distinct characteristics of distance, latency and robustness. SDI (BNC coaxial) is the broadcast standard because it allows cables of up to 100 m without amplification and is immune to mechanical interference. HDMI is practical for consumer sources, but limited to ~15 m and prone to contact failures. For distances over 100 m, fiber optics eliminate the restriction with practically zero latency. NDI transports professional video over Gigabit IP networks, ideal for existing IT infrastructures, but requires bandwidth planning (up to 125 Mbps per stream) and VLAN isolation to ensure stability in live events.

76f7fd6a-ab89-43fa-8d11-89c2d75bd095.pergunta: What is the central difference between live video and post-production?
76f7fd6a-ab89-43fa-8d11-89c2d75bd095.explicacao: In live, the mistake goes on air instantly; there is no rendering and no second chance, which is why redundancy is vital.

options.229afc1b-6a10-49f4-976c-47f41de6eab8: Live is processed and displayed in real time, with no redoing

c5cd3c1d-7dad-416f-8bab-8c049210b144.pergunta: What does the switcher operator (switcher/TD) do?
c5cd3c1d-7dad-416f-8bab-8c049210b144.explicacao: They receive several sources and select in real time what goes to the program and the LED wall.

options.14f3f9a8-79e1-489e-a8e0-3388239c84a3: Decides which source goes on air and makes the transitions
options.32a965e8-6617-45b2-bbfc-035c6d4ab091: Edits the video the next day

e0985be5-6a3b-4932-86a2-11a5a47bdedb.pergunta: Why is drawing the signal flow important?
e0985be5-6a3b-4932-86a2-11a5a47bdedb.explicacao: The signal flow is the map that guides both the setup and the troubleshooting of failures.

options.1aa6b1e4-7f12-45fa-a083-4bc6c56c276f: To diagnose problems and plan the setup

aaff7252-4cfd-4846-bee1-fe61b2269329.pergunta: What is the native resolution of a display?
aaff7252-4cfd-4846-bee1-fe61b2269329.explicacao: It is the display's physical pixel count; sending another resolution forces rescaling.

options.012cc068-cc5a-40b7-a124-2be2998011d3: The physical number of pixels on the panel
options.cad022e8-3ed1-43c9-9d7a-cdcfd0225b1d: The highest resolution it accepts on the input

aca2c79b-2157-4746-868d-0bacb7b84e53.pergunta: Why prefer a progressive signal over interlaced in LED/VJ?
aca2c79b-2157-4746-868d-0bacb7b84e53.explicacao: Interlaced splits the frame into fields and produces jaggies in scenes with motion.

d4ce5b94-fffc-428e-830a-4644ff22757a.pergunta: A 24p video on a wall at 60 Hz can cause what?
d4ce5b94-fffc-428e-830a-4644ff22757a.explicacao: Converting the 24→60 cadence carelessly generates judder; the ideal is to render at 60p.

a9399d83-371d-4e64-9b43-f0328194e0c2.pergunta: What does a circle turning into an ellipse in the test pattern indicate?
a9399d83-371d-4e64-9b43-f0328194e0c2.explicacao: Shape distortion indicates that the aspect ratio does not match the destination's.

0172f10b-1b40-41ec-a57f-9bc91e9c5b7e.pergunta: Which component is most critical for a VJ PC?
0172f10b-1b40-41ec-a57f-9bc91e9c5b7e.explicacao: The GPU accelerates playback, effects and multiple outputs, defining how many layers you can run.

86ef4086-62d9-4d75-9de3-c1419655f5de.pergunta: Why not use an HDD for live playback?
86ef4086-62d9-4d75-9de3-c1419655f5de.explicacao: 4K video requires very fast reads; an HDD causes stutters. Use NVMe/SSD.

options.99bb5995-5c61-4e0a-aa06-cba1b2918e8a: It doesn't have enough read speed for high-resolution video

341230ee-e736-4292-9aad-904d33968995.pergunta: What is a Datapath FX4 for?
341230ee-e736-4292-9aad-904d33968995.explicacao: It expands one output into up to 4 displays treated as a single area, ideal for multi-screen.

options.9966f62e-a7f8-4832-9f3d-66090a53cb2b: Split one GPU output into several screens/regions

51a9612e-e3cc-4461-b24e-96bf4cb340c0.pergunta: What should you do in the OS before the show?
51a9612e-e3cc-4461-b24e-96bf4cb340c0.explicacao: Any OS interruption can crash the show; disable anything that can pause the machine.

8b950adc-62c2-42ed-9b54-af9de944b28b.pergunta: Which connection is standard for professional cameras and switchers because it locks and runs long distances?
8b950adc-62c2-42ed-9b54-af9de944b28b.explicacao: SDI over coaxial with BNC runs 100+ m and has a firm connector, ideal for broadcast.

e1fb05ac-3504-48d8-ad19-48f1899d8bf4.pergunta: To carry video hundreds of meters without interference, the best option is:
e1fb05ac-3504-48d8-ad19-48f1899d8bf4.explicacao: Fiber is immune to electrical noise and covers very large distances.

a179c6af-2a7e-4acf-989e-75336f64e268.pergunta: What is the main weak point of HDMI at events?
a179c6af-2a7e-4acf-989e-75336f64e268.explicacao: HDMI degrades with distance and the connector comes loose easily; on stage prefer SDI.

options.e03c4104-164d-47b2-95a9-4b233c7103d7: Distance and a connector without a lock

d977aa27-c62d-4efe-a7ef-dc47e5efd498.pergunta: What travels over an RJ45 network cable in video?
d977aa27-c62d-4efe-a7ef-dc47e5efd498.explicacao: A network cable carries LED control (Art-Net/sACN), video over NDI and HDMI over HDBaseT.

e5798e87-a3f6-467d-a8b5-46b3a6b73189.pergunta: What is the difference between a splitter (DA) and a matrix?
e5798e87-a3f6-467d-a8b5-46b3a6b73189.explicacao: The distributor replicates identically; the matrix chooses which input goes to which output.

options.3a836e61-4170-4099-b647-b59ceadb8585: A splitter copies the source to several outputs; a matrix routes inputs to destinations

bf4918bf-fe43-4b9b-ac24-0fb807f9aec1.pergunta: When do you need a scaler?
bf4918bf-fe43-4b9b-ac24-0fb807f9aec1.explicacao: The scaler converts resolution and cadence, matching incompatible signals.

options.da075c5d-31fa-4f8e-b96e-a1a93e1a1f82: When the source's resolution/frame rate doesn't match the destination

90be5033-bf0c-47fa-8680-b109bea53e90.pergunta: A passive adapter is used to:
90be5033-bf0c-47fa-8680-b109bea53e90.explicacao: An adapter only changes the connector; incompatible signals require an active converter.

options.76968ac9-88ab-431b-9fbb-fa473f528d9c: Only change the connector between compatible signals

fa06ff72-ad21-4e9c-8629-3d832ee32ebc.pergunta: What can cause a black screen with streaming/Blu-ray material?
fa06ff72-ad21-4e9c-8629-3d832ee32ebc.explicacao: HDCP protection doesn't pass through many professional devices, causing a black screen.

options.c45e7846-cc71-4bd5-9deb-1438261b95e4: HDCP not negotiated in the professional chain

ece2cccc-3621-4eae-a1ba-5cb0e06d08af.pergunta: Why does emissive LED dominate stage walls?
ece2cccc-3621-4eae-a1ba-5cb0e06d08af.explicacao: LED is modular, very bright and has no bezels between modules, forming a continuous image.

options.fcbbc2b7-9524-417d-bc3b-12854fb8e85f: High brightness, modular and no visible seams

77d97cff-444a-4d38-8490-f55e617581d2.pergunta: What defines the required pixel pitch?
77d97cff-444a-4d38-8490-f55e617581d2.explicacao: The closer the audience, the smaller the pitch so they don't see the dots.

options.dafb2272-ad3f-4127-899f-c43248365e19: The audience's distance from the screen

076b1b03-4bc7-4aaa-813d-2ba3b9a87a87.pergunta: For a wall filmed by a camera, what is critical?
076b1b03-4bc7-4aaa-813d-2ba3b9a87a87.explicacao: A low refresh appears as black bands in the camera's image; use a high refresh.

options.85ed5b87-85a2-4981-af20-41a1a03caba5: High refresh rate to avoid scan lines

e4c48b9c-3c40-452d-a859-55a7a496a876.pergunta: What is the disadvantage of an LCD videowall for continuous imagery?
e4c48b9c-3c40-452d-a859-55a7a496a876.explicacao: The LCD bezels create visible lines; LED has no seams.

1f8c3217-f136-46af-99b2-4a899b979f55.explicacao: Pitch is the distance in mm between LEDs; smaller pitch = more resolution per meter.

6fbf71f1-cde5-4315-86f6-374bd3ca749b.pergunta: What is the role of the LED processor (Novastar/Colorlight)?
6fbf71f1-cde5-4315-86f6-374bd3ca749b.explicacao: It scales to the wall's native resolution and sends the data to the cabinets, adjusting brightness/color.

options.4c9e01ed-feff-4410-b117-42b0349c094a: Receive the video, scale it and distribute it to the cabinets

7dba57b1-9b33-4592-a2f0-143aeed09f1b.pergunta: What is mapping the screen in the software (NovaLCT) for?
7dba57b1-9b33-4592-a2f0-143aeed09f1b.explicacao: The mapping tells the system the cabling order, positioning each cabinet in the image.

options.98f279f1-c004-4289-a961-c18a23aec3aa: Change the color of the building
options.6516591a-a4b5-4b67-9a14-70af26c77fdf: Tell the order and position of the cabinets so the image appears correctly

68bdb3bc-2d21-4c3a-b7d7-4433675ad1b4.pergunta: How do you calculate the resolution of a wall?
68bdb3bc-2d21-4c3a-b7d7-4433675ad1b4.explicacao: Each cabinet has a fixed resolution; the wall is the sum in width x height.

options.1c65fce1-de19-48e9-9b5e-d94eccf741bb: By the length of the cable
options.1f770a8a-47db-43f1-804e-d1fe19b6f4f7: By multiplying the pixels per cabinet by the number of cabinets

ed56c5bf-6881-4664-83d2-cf0d94332b86.pergunta: What does a grid test pattern reveal on the wall?
ed56c5bf-6881-4664-83d2-cf0d94332b86.explicacao: The grid highlights mapping failures, dead modules and color differences.

options.e8517ce5-850e-4057-b8ba-b047927f1ef0: Dead modules, cabinets out of order and uneven color

8b9b8890-446b-4709-8985-963f85b3db78.pergunta: What determines the amount of lumens needed?
8b9b8890-446b-4709-8985-963f85b3db78.explicacao: A larger image and a brighter environment require more lumens so it doesn't 'wash out'.

options.954cb035-f9cd-48ac-8f32-50f0f72c9ba4: The image size and the ambient light

d21fd02f-0f85-4653-9b17-5ee81c99b68e.explicacao: With the projector behind the translucent screen, no one in front creates a shadow.

options.2a572aa9-8c46-44ba-8ffe-dd6e75a018ae: It eliminates the shadows of people passing in front

3118ab28-f558-4607-a8fb-83983d422af7.pergunta: What is the throw ratio for?
3118ab28-f558-4607-a8fb-83983d422af7.explicacao: It indicates which lens to use according to the available distance and the image size.

options.0c58686f-3d86-47b6-b381-30f73ccc3a81: To relate the projector's distance to the image width in order to choose the lens

7529c981-3c0f-4561-b7d7-22b873571e3e.explicacao: Keystone corrects distortion by software, but loses sharpness; prefer lens shift/positioning.

2a50d57d-ba65-46c7-88f0-693bb051aa18.pergunta: What is the difference between Program and Preview?
2a50d57d-ba65-46c7-88f0-693bb051aa18.explicacao: You select in Preview and take it on air via cut/transition, avoiding cutting blind.

options.2aa6614f-ea9c-4a65-b3fa-ed9ffa845aac: Program is on air; Preview prepares the next source

c92aea49-b6df-4447-8ed0-2400642a5224.explicacao: A cut is the hard cut; dissolve and wipe are gradual transitions.

a3bd2af2-fef4-4360-883a-1c469ff7af47.pergunta: What is the DSK (downstream keyer) for?
a3bd2af2-fef4-4360-883a-1c469ff7af47.explicacao: The DSK applies graphics over the program without interfering with the main transitions.

options.b41b74ea-3c71-446a-aabe-9a17081f26a4: To overlay graphics/lower-thirds on top of everything

168f5e03-cdd9-4bf9-8b17-19cf43e9ee82.explicacao: The tally light comes on the camera that's on PGM, warning the camera operator.

0d70a20f-2df5-4b46-b005-167154bc5f94.pergunta: When should you use the Roland's per-input scaler?
0d70a20f-2df5-4b46-b005-167154bc5f94.explicacao: The scaler matches inputs of different resolutions to the switcher format.

options.087a740e-df35-4d2f-bfb0-1064c8dbdd28: When the sources have resolutions different from the switcher format

f686de1d-1c68-4971-bced-4386e30a668a.explicacao: It's a stack of layers, each playing one clip at a time, forming the composition.

56c1e4aa-c670-469e-ab55-52987f56d367.explicacao: Decks are pages of clips; switching decks swaps the whole set at once.

options.46eaf7a6-f0e5-4cab-9a57-269957f1a463: Organizing sets of clips by show moment

e4d4bdb8-8c21-4339-98b5-8b4bcff662b8.pergunta: What does Audio FX / BPM do in Resolume?
e4d4bdb8-8c21-4339-98b5-8b4bcff662b8.explicacao: The visuals and effects follow the tempo/audio, pulsing with the beat.

options.9623bdd3-a8b8-46eb-bc46-1e77216b54c3: Syncs speed and effects with the music

cfce182a-6aad-4e4d-88b4-b4db8d494fd1.pergunta: Where do you map the output to the big screen in Resolume Arena?
cfce182a-6aad-4e4d-88b4-b4db8d494fd1.explicacao: Advanced Output defines the output resolution, slicing, warp and mask for the destination.

61e5ea65-b5d2-49b2-b13a-85bb0495820b.explicacao: MIDI gives tactile live control, essential for playing visuals like an instrument.

options.f93f5be6-4ca4-47e4-a522-1b1d262a5456: To trigger clips and adjust parameters with your hands, without the mouse

2ea81193-47c9-4242-9739-1f9b36fc9660.pergunta: What sets a media server apart from a simple software VJ?
2ea81193-47c9-4242-9739-1f9b36fc9660.explicacao: Media servers focus on timed, synchronized playback across several screens with redundancy.

options.758e31a2-ee83-43e4-8648-611b520b1f4a: Reliability, precise sync and multiple outputs controlled by timecode

4a71d1ff-3d2e-4ac5-82f3-7d11c1853c7e.explicacao: It's the time reference that makes the content trigger on the exact frame along with the music and the light.

options.e6a1130e-42cc-474a-ae20-e41585b1028a: A HH:MM:SS:FF clock that synchronizes video, audio and light

1d86d9f4-d0a0-46a2-998d-48b83b1ae83a.pergunta: What is the pixel canvas for?
1d86d9f4-d0a0-46a2-998d-48b83b1ae83a.explicacao: It's the virtual screen where the content is assembled and sliced for the physical destinations.

options.4277b000-f922-4628-b3c1-a1ad46c26c6b: To position the content and crop regions for each screen/projector

9674d1bc-374b-4f86-8086-4ff82a70537d.explicacao: Redundancy ensures the show continues even if the main server goes down.

options.86ba4570-aa10-457a-bfd7-179aa35bf6a5: To take over automatically if the main one fails (failover)

4590a522-1eac-4536-a4b8-4bcd01139f6e.explicacao: Warp adjusts corners/mesh so the content sticks to the real shape of the object.

options.f165d1a1-4779-4d13-89f1-8ac7f46a6c17: Deforming the image to fit the perspective of the surface

f7f7f7a5-0592-4af8-9ba9-d6f11cbed2f8.explicacao: The mask prevents the light from spilling outside the area that should be projected.

options.cbc801d3-f62d-4a06-a952-d82553b0e350: To crop the projection so the light only hits the desired surface

7ed0ab7b-f61c-428b-a26a-b4d77ab9c1c8.explicacao: The blend mixes the overlapping edges of several projectors, with no visible line.

options.3ffb7408-a43e-4710-a947-4e88ff1cc90f: Smoothing the seam between projectors to look like a single image

8aeaed6a-4097-434a-aca3-d1745b2353b8.pergunta: Why create a template of the surface?
8aeaed6a-4097-434a-aca3-d1745b2353b8.explicacao: The template ensures the content respects the edges, windows and shapes of the object.

options.fa972268-412b-437e-b728-bfeac1121551: So the designer produces content aligned to the real geometry

9754432c-3b7d-4413-91d1-f07dafb697d4.pergunta: Which codec is optimized for live playback in Resolume?
9754432c-3b7d-4413-91d1-f07dafb697d4.explicacao: HAP/DXV are light on the GPU, ideal for many live layers in Resolume.

fd74f19d-5161-416d-982d-14c28c051ed4.pergunta: When do you need an alpha channel on export?
fd74f19d-5161-416d-982d-14c28c051ed4.explicacao: Logos and elements on top require alpha (ProRes 4444, PNG sequence) to preserve transparency.

options.bea3d86a-de78-42cc-8c14-c41e0c9d516b: When the element will be overlaid and needs transparency

5ef26f0b-3d8e-4598-897f-868269f91c1e.explicacao: Continuous backgrounds need the last frame to match the first, with no visible jump.

options.ed4c33f4-ede7-4e96-9fee-2d86a692f205: Content whose start and end match to repeat without a jump

dc87d3a0-473f-439f-877c-bf41fc9cb9ce.pergunta: Why export at the exact resolution of the destination?
dc87d3a0-473f-439f-877c-bf41fc9cb9ce.explicacao: Matching the resolution avoids scaling on stage, which adds latency and blurs the image.

options.02beed03-14fa-4443-ae24-9d0ebee8af75: To avoid rescaling and loss in live playback

9a96a22b-30ec-4f1b-8a6a-774717b3cade.explicacao: The container packages video+audio; the codec defines how the video is compressed.

options.ddc5767a-2d56-451f-b35d-91444e62ed2b: The container (MOV/MP4) wraps; the codec is the internal compression

c5b3c3c2-301c-4c24-8206-2cd7135338d6.pergunta: Why avoid heavily compressed H.264 in many live layers?
c5b3c3c2-301c-4c24-8206-2cd7135338d6.explicacao: Inter-frame H.264 demands more processing; intra-frame HAP/ProRes are lighter live.

options.74e26396-a966-49d3-a9d7-c5ba9d43167b: It's inter-frame and heavy on the CPU; intra-frame codecs run better

034bb7d5-1850-43d1-9071-97425387dcd4.pergunta: What does the 3-2-1 backup rule say?
034bb7d5-1850-43d1-9071-97425387dcd4.explicacao: It's the data redundancy best practice that protects against losses.

2e5ccacc-12d3-459f-8393-bf3a70d6ec79.pergunta: Why transcode everything to a standard before the event?
2e5ccacc-12d3-459f-8393-bf3a70d6ec79.explicacao: Standardizing resolution/codec ensures smooth playback with no surprises live.

options.2cf1007d-609b-4797-91cd-e046183121e6: To avoid rescaling and incompatible codecs freezing on stage

5d63f9f7-1129-480a-bd13-0d79b32cd85d.explicacao: Without understanding the briefing, you deliver something technically correct but wrong for the event.

opcoes.58feda6a-926f-4673-8a57-b6a6e9495f92: The document that defines the goal, audience and client expectations

1c091ed2-e861-4448-be34-8709246caf86.pergunta: What is the technical rider for?
1c091ed2-e861-4448-be34-8709246caf86.explicacao: The rider communicates everything you need, in advance, so the setup can happen.

opcoes.bb9d2ee8-9673-4965-9791-98538b10bf90: Listing equipment, power, cabling and requirements for production

392cae13-8614-451a-898f-1638c39401a2.pergunta: What does 'VT' mean in event jargon?
392cae13-8614-451a-898f-1638c39401a2.explicacao: VT is the clip/video that will be triggered; 'roll VT' means play that content.

bb032e5f-f577-4872-bbe3-9b04d008f54f.explicacao: Third-party music, videos and fonts may require a usage license.

opcoes.94ce1eda-f0a0-4d16-8b16-351846128ff5: To avoid legal problems with third-party material

1f55737e-089c-4197-9d0b-bcd4dda97dc8.pergunta: What is the role of the signal map in documentation?
1f55737e-089c-4197-9d0b-bcd4dda97dc8.explicacao: The cabling diagram guides setup and lets you find faults quickly.

opcoes.2c13eb82-47b9-496e-b840-d1d4b2449b44: Speeding up setup and troubleshooting by showing the chain from source to destination

acb40e34-d4c5-467b-89ae-3aee151b602e.pergunta: In a cut console's workflow, what does PGM mean?
acb40e34-d4c5-467b-89ae-3aee151b602e.explicacao: PGM (Program) is the signal that's on air; PVW (Preview/Preset) is what you prepare before putting it on air.

opcoes.b2db503f-3baf-4d09-ba2c-c9e82e55506e: The signal that's ON AIR, going to recording/stream/screen
opcoes.80cc293e-5f72-4eb3-8cba-b7c7f6f36933: The source being prepared to come in

e3efe055-94b4-4971-a49d-2f0c9160c157.pergunta: Which keyer is best suited for a permanent logo over everything on screen?
e3efe055-94b4-4971-a49d-2f0c9160c157.explicacao: The DSK is applied as a final layer, over the entire composition, ideal for a fixed logo/credit.

99f0e699-53e1-4618-a608-ed51c2a7442f.pergunta: Why is the ATEM Mini popular in lives and podcasts?
99f0e699-53e1-4618-a608-ed51c2a7442f.explicacao: The ATEM Mini outputs over USB as a webcam and mixes HDMI inputs, perfect for lives and video podcasts.

opcoes.1121cc33-f752-452d-bb50-bf6d57869562: It appears as a webcam via USB and cuts HDMI sources
opcoes.957fdf29-fb69-4aef-a949-5dc25948965f: It does soft-edge blending of projectors

12996259-b0cc-45c2-b3b3-697462cde97d.pergunta: What feature makes the Roland V-800HD suitable for corporate events with mixed sources?
12996259-b0cc-45c2-b3b3-697462cde97d.explicacao: Each V-800HD input has its own scaler, so a 1080p camera and a laptop at another resolution coexist without external converters.

opcoes.63b9bd53-cceb-4e47-92f4-aff263ec03b7: Independent scaler on each input, accepting different formats

d9f13fa2-3392-4369-a68a-730c6bfa4c7c.pergunta: What is an AUX output on a switcher?
d9f13fa2-3392-4369-a68a-730c6bfa4c7c.explicacao: The AUX is an auxiliary output routable independently of PGM, common for feeding a screen or recording an isolated source.

opcoes.bf44d957-b333-403e-843e-10060566ceda: An independent auxiliary output, used for the screen or ISO

4026ad09-fd64-44f7-a5a3-74b3f1eaba7d.pergunta: What's the difference between output mapping and projection mapping?
4026ad09-fd64-44f7-a5a3-74b3f1eaba7d.explicacao: Output mapping organizes which piece goes to which output; projection mapping warps/aligns the image with the physical geometry of the surface.

opcoes.3072a650-f97e-41da-a4c0-ee318686b2ae: Output mapping is exclusive to Watchout
opcoes.9462b2da-25ec-4419-bb32-8fcfe0d07861: Projection mapping only works on LED
opcoes.766cb812-2063-4168-a0d8-4b0da8116529: Output mapping routes parts of the composition to the outputs; projection mapping deforms the image to fit 3D surfaces

31c3d625-615d-4e31-a3d4-987044b347c2.pergunta: Which system is typically timeline-based and used in museums and fixed installations?
31c3d625-615d-4e31-a3d4-987044b347c2.explicacao: Watchout is a reference in timeline-based multi-screen display, common in exhibitions and permanent installations.

624bd40a-3a23-4a2f-8682-029bc41da58f.explicacao: Soft-edge blending creates a gradient in the projector overlap area to form a single image with no visible seam.

opcoes.a264319c-bc6b-4c62-a240-85d3e09af03f: The blend band between overlapping projectors that hides the seam

7f8c2370-0796-4cc5-a731-e514c74ba25d.pergunta: What is the disguise Designer's '3D virtual stage' for?
7f8c2370-0796-4cc5-a731-e514c74ba25d.explicacao: disguise models the scene in 3D in Designer, letting you map content onto the real set and enable xR/virtual production workflows.

opcoes.807b9e1d-4427-435a-999c-579db93ce21b: Modeling the set/LED in the software and mapping content onto the model, the basis of xR and virtual production

330eeadf-cc44-4a2e-b21d-53f14bf5083c.pergunta: Why add a backup server with failover in big events?
330eeadf-cc44-4a2e-b21d-53f14bf5083c.explicacao: Redundancy with failover guarantees the show's continuity: if the main server goes down, the synced backup takes over without interruption.

opcoes.c58db4ca-3979-495c-8164-39cf26b52442: So a backup server takes over if the main one fails, avoiding a black screen

b8addb99-3035-49ed-851b-df5731c1cf2e.pergunta: What does a smaller pixel pitch mean (e.g., P2 vs P6)?
b8addb99-3035-49ed-851b-df5731c1cf2e.explicacao: Smaller pitch = pixels closer together = more resolution and the possibility of close-up viewing, usually at a higher cost.

opcoes.90306e17-8719-4c5b-8aea-1977c7893829: Less resolution and long-distance viewing only
opcoes.afa07598-17a5-4fae-a25d-aee31c7923f7: More pixels per meter, more resolution, and the audience can be closer

e237ecf4-7608-4b92-beab-f6cc27625f15.explicacao: The ~1 m per 1 mm of pitch rule indicates a P4 starts to look adequate at around 4 m from the audience.

05dc88c9-1ed1-4574-87e5-1189abd4287b.pergunta: Why does a high refresh rate matter when a camera is filming the panel?
05dc88c9-1ed1-4574-87e5-1189abd4287b.explicacao: Cameras capture the flicker of low-refresh panels as bands; a high refresh (3840 Hz+) eliminates that artifact in the image.

opcoes.33f6ec82-968e-4dec-935d-39c82d9079f3: To avoid scanlines/flicker (bands) that appear on the video

b5244f59-1fce-4dcf-bf7f-4fa4c9fcf286.pergunta: What is the role of the processor/sending card (e.g., NovaStar)?
b5244f59-1fce-4dcf-bf7f-4fa4c9fcf286.explicacao: The processor receives the signal, processes it and maps the image onto the cabinets, sending the data to the receiving cards.

opcoes.f83c913d-7ef2-46e8-9fce-b5c696832d1b: Receiving the video, scaling/adjusting and mapping the image pixel by pixel onto the cabinets

9f606238-4fb7-48f6-b413-7a0a2548d7f3.pergunta: For an outdoor screen with the audience at 15 m in strong sun, what makes most sense?
9f606238-4fb7-48f6-b413-7a0a2548d7f3.explicacao: At 15 m, a larger pitch (P6/P10) is enough, and high brightness (4000-6000 nits) is needed to beat the sunlight.

63a7ef77-9ada-4cef-8793-9114270e8445.pergunta: What increases the need for lumens in a project the most?
63a7ef77-9ada-4cef-8793-9114270e8445.explicacao: The larger the screen and the more ambient light, the more lumens needed for a visible, contrasted image.

opcoes.07592161-b3f7-49f5-b094-4f1e5c9e1965: Large screen and lots of ambient light
opcoes.c467bf0a-e86d-4e68-b93e-80f81bd76a17: Small screen in a dark room
opcoes.9f33f21a-14f3-4aae-9f6e-246e9a0c2f09: Using laser instead of a lamp

3a2f495e-fe82-4f7a-8f3e-a932255ba93b.pergunta: What is the main advantage of a laser projector over a lamp one?
3a2f495e-fe82-4f7a-8f3e-a932255ba93b.explicacao: Laser offers ~20,000 h, constant color and brightness, instant on and 360° operation, ideal for mapping.

opcoes.76111a06-77b9-4b2c-8ea5-74fd81e8cc75: Long lifespan (~20,000 h), stable color/brightness and operation at any angle
opcoes.6a237ce8-510e-4eba-8174-04fdde7a330a: It does away with the media server

f6d9cd84-db14-4147-9cbd-90d2abd60b3c.explicacao: Throw ratio = distance/image width; it defines which lens to use according to the available space.

opcoes.87171fb9-8f2a-4e93-a20c-0acd5d2cebab: The projector-to-screen distance divided by the image width

de6d75a6-89d6-41d8-af76-c794a8003d41.pergunta: Why size lumens with margin in building mapping?
de6d75a6-89d6-41d8-af76-c794a8003d41.explicacao: Colored/dark walls and losses from distance and lens reduce useful brightness, so you project with margin (and/or stacking).

opcoes.3218c69c-4e1a-41c4-8d73-7292f56b7395: Because non-white surfaces and distance reduce the perceived brightness

6578ba54-1851-4511-bd40-10d5702ef212.pergunta: To join several projectors into a single panoramic image, you use:
6578ba54-1851-4511-bd40-10d5702ef212.explicacao: Edge blending mixes the overlap areas between projectors, forming a single continuous image with no visible seam.

56a85b00-1312-4805-a341-b55d5063b1ed.explicacao: IMAG (Image Magnification) is displaying the stage scene enlarged, live, on the screen for those far away.

opcoes.23455416-d1e0-47d6-9320-7e4aeb529c24: Enlarging the stage image live on the screen for the distant audience

fdda35b1-f120-47aa-bafb-cd7e9293e3a0.pergunta: What is a capture card like the Elgato Cam Link for?
fdda35b1-f120-47aa-bafb-cd7e9293e3a0.explicacao: The capture card converts the camera signal into a source (webcam/UVC) read by the streaming/recording software.

opcoes.77bcbb5b-cf40-4af5-babf-e1c50507afeb: Converting the camera's HDMI/SDI into a video source the computer understands

cd154d72-0b04-406e-a9bd-fbea97c7a36d.pergunta: What is the main advantage of a PTZ camera in lean production?
cd154d72-0b04-406e-a9bd-fbea97c7a36d.explicacao: PTZ are motorized and controlled remotely via VISCA/NDI; with presets, one operator commands several positions.

opcoes.85534fbf-6330-4031-858d-05a5833a9187: It can be controlled remotely with presets, letting one person operate several cameras
opcoes.c16737bd-713f-494f-a7cc-e0cc9361cd0f: It has the best sensor on the market
opcoes.815958c7-b226-47d5-8b0d-a184986c3dc7: It does away with the switcher

31075745-6f26-4b6f-bf64-aa8c939d84de.pergunta: What is 'clean output' on a camera?
31075745-6f26-4b6f-bf64-aa8c939d84de.explicacao: Clean output delivers only the image, with no menu overlays, essential for sending to the switcher/screen.

opcoes.54389a6a-fc8d-4715-8aa3-cd0f89b7415b: Output with the menus and icons on screen
opcoes.86f16363-bcfd-4c2a-958b-b1791c6df4b3: A clean video output, with no overlaid menus/information

69bc2765-9f01-446f-833f-8c5005f2a282.pergunta: When is SDI output preferable to HDMI on a camera?
69bc2765-9f01-446f-833f-8c5005f2a282.explicacao: SDI uses BNC, supports long cables and a locked connection, being the professional standard for distance and reliability.

opcoes.dbe44a97-bef1-4885-b9d8-ac4ced6b3dbf: On long, locked cables, the event/broadcast standard

e453567c-110d-43de-91ba-5d7d53c83bc8.pergunta: Why is SDI preferred over HDMI at events with distance?
e453567c-110d-43de-91ba-5d7d53c83bc8.explicacao: SDI uses a locked BNC and coaxial cable, transporting video over long distances reliably, the broadcast/event standard.

opcoes.fdc2b834-eb99-42cf-84be-f6d3caa40ee4: It uses a BNC connector that locks and reaches tens to 100+ meters without compression

dbc56d3b-8890-49bc-88b7-6a673c61777a.pergunta: A speaker arrives with a laptop that only has HDMI, but the switcher is SDI. What solves it?
dbc56d3b-8890-49bc-88b7-6a673c61777a.explicacao: An HDMI→SDI converter adapts the laptop signal to the SDI switcher immediately.

opcoes.bae30258-5d6a-4247-bdae-c2854f1dc575: An HDMI to SDI converter (e.g., Blackmagic Micro Converter)

b540e8d3-c6b6-49cf-b719-7c49daf444cf.pergunta: When is optical fiber (opticalCON) the best choice?
b540e8d3-c6b6-49cf-b719-7c49daf444cf.explicacao: Fiber transports video over very long distances without interference; opticalCON is the robust connector that's the stage standard.

opcoes.995bcccf-02f6-4e8b-bc0a-aa94c2c6afbc: For very long runs (hundreds of meters to km), immune to interference

5a798e4d-b3b5-45d8-a2fa-465dbe33c798.pergunta: What is the main advantage of NDI?
5a798e4d-b3b5-45d8-a2fa-465dbe33c798.explicacao: NDI carries video/audio/control over an IP network, allowing many sources on simple cabling — as long as the network is well sized.

opcoes.446b7c4e-c7ad-4299-a3de-d55096f82fc7: It transports many video sources over one Ethernet network cable, simplifying cabling
opcoes.60a09e49-64ab-472e-8049-13650db9360d: It does away with any network

79931d31-5e3c-4a3d-8168-41f3a64b5c3f.pergunta: What is the most common cause of failure when integrating different sources?
79931d31-5e3c-4a3d-8168-41f3a64b5c3f.explicacao: Different resolution, frame rate and color space between sources take the image down; standardize the format or use a converter/scaler.

opcoes.98daac73-7376-42a5-93a4-e3a3ca3e878c: An SDI cable that's too locked

86afa2ea-8e43-4458-96fc-7a12b6b6dec1.titulo: Qué hace un técnico de vídeo/VJ en eventos en vivo
86afa2ea-8e43-4458-96fc-7a12b6b6dec1.conteudo_rico:
Para empezar: Antes de tocar cualquier cable, debes entender qué se entrega al público y cuál es tu responsabilidad en la cadena.

El técnico de vídeo en vivo es el profesional que garantiza que la imagen correcta llegue a la pantalla correcta, en el momento correcto y con la calidad correcta. A diferencia del editor de posproducción (que trabaja offline, con tiempo), el técnico de vídeo en vivo opera en tiempo real: no hay segunda oportunidad. En esta lección entenderás el alcance de la función, los roles que existen dentro de una operación (VJ, switcher/mezclador de corte, operador de media server, operador de LED, ingeniero de señal) y cómo se articulan en un evento real, desde un culto hasta un show de arena.

  • VJ (Video Jockey): Profesional que crea y manipula contenido visual en tiempo real, generalmente sincronizado con la música, usando software como Resolume Arena. Es el equivalente visual del DJ: mezcla clips, aplica efectos y reacciona al ambiente. Actúa mucho en fiestas, raves, shows electrónicos y escenografía de escenario.
  • Operador de corte (switcher/TD): También llamado Director Técnico en broadcast. Recibe varias fuentes (cámaras, diapositivas, vídeos) y decide cuál va a la pantalla principal y al muro de LED, haciendo cortes y transiciones. Es la función central en conferencias, cultos, transmisiones y eventos corporativos.
  • Operador de media server / playback: Responsable de reproducir contenido preproducido (aperturas, cortinillas, lower-thirds, fondos de escenario) de forma fiable y sincronizada. Usa servidores como Watchout, Resolume, Modulo Pi, disguise (d3) o PlaybackPro.
  • Operador de LED / ingeniero de señal: Se encarga del muro físico de LED: configura el procesador de LED, mapea los módulos, garantiza resolución y calibración de color. En estructuras grandes, es una especialidad propia.
  • Diferencia entre en vivo y posproducción: En la posproducción renderizas, revisas y rehaces. En vivo, todo se procesa y se muestra en fracciones de segundo (la baja latencia es crítica). El error sale al aire al instante — por eso la redundancia, el ensayo y la checklist son obligatorios.
  • Flujo de señal (signal flow): Es el camino que recorre la imagen: fuente (cámara/computadora) → conversor/distribuidor → switcher/media server → procesador → pantalla/proyector. Entender esta cadena es la base de todo diagnóstico de problemas.
  • Atribuciones típicas del puesto: Mezcla de vídeo en vivo, mapping, efectos y transiciones, integración con audio (timecode/SMPTE), control por MIDI y OSC, soporte para múltiples pantallas, compatibilidad con diversas fuentes y hardware, y flexibilidad para reconfigurar rápido en campo.

Equipos / detalles

  • Portátil/PC de operación: Máquina robusta (i7/i9 o Ryzen 7/9, 32 GB RAM, GPU dedicada) ejecutando el software de VJ o playback. Es la 'consola' del operador moderno.
  • Switcher de vídeo (ej.: Blackmagic ATEM, Roland V-60HD): Mesa de corte que recibe múltiples fuentes y entrega el programa final. Usado por el operador de corte.
  • Monitor de preview/program: Pantallas de referencia para que el operador vea lo que está al aire (Program) y lo que viene a continuación (Preview), evitando cortar 'a ciegas'.

Paso a paso

  1. 1. Mapea los roles del evento: Pregunta al productor: ¿habrá cámara en vivo? ¿Muro de LED o proyección? ¿Contenido preproducido? Esto define cuántas funciones y qué equipo serán necesarios.
  2. 2. Dibuja el flujo de señal en papel: Enumera todas las fuentes (PCs, cámaras, reproductores) y todos los destinos (muro, proyector, grabación, streaming). Únelos con flechas. Ese diagrama se convierte en tu mapa de montaje.
  3. 3. Define la resolución de trabajo: Elige la resolución común del evento (ej.: 1920x1080@60) y asegúrate de que todas las fuentes y destinos la soporten, para evitar reescalados y retrasos.

En la práctica: En un culto mediano, el operador de corte se sienta en una mesa Roland V-60HD recibiendo 3 cámaras y la computadora de letras/diapositivas; corta entre cámaras durante la alabanza y abre la letra en el muro en el momento justo. El muro es LED P3 alimentado por un procesador Novastar. Entender que esa computadora de diapositivas es solo una 'fuente' más en la cadena cambia por completo la forma en que diagnosticas una pantalla en negro.

Resumen

  • El técnico de vídeo en vivo entrega la imagen correcta, en la pantalla correcta, en tiempo real, sin segunda oportunidad.
  • Existen roles distintos: VJ, operador de corte, operador de media server y operador de LED.
  • Todo comienza por entender el flujo de señal — fuente, procesamiento y destino.

El Técnico de Vídeo/VJ en Eventos en Vivo

El técnico de vídeo es responsable de toda la cadena de señal visual en un evento: de la cámara al display, pasando por el switcher, el servidor de playback y los grafismos. Opera el switcher de vídeo para elegir, en tiempo real, qué fuente está al aire — cámara, VT, animación o pantalla en negro —, controla transiciones y mantiene la continuidad visual del espectáculo. El VJ creativo amplía este papel al generar y manipular contenido visual en vivo, sincronizado con la música o el guion. En ambos casos, la preparación comienza horas antes del show: verificación de cables y resolución, carga de la playlist, calibración de proyectores y ensayo técnico con todo el equipo. La comunicación precisa y rápida con el director, los operadores de cámara y los técnicos de escenario es tan esencial como cualquier habilidad técnica.

fe816937-1c38-4c2b-92d2-f03af6cfb138.titulo: Resolución, frame rate, píxeles y relación de aspecto
fe816937-1c38-4c2b-92d2-f03af6cfb138.conteudo_rico:
Para empezar: Toda imagen digital es una cuadrícula de píxeles que se actualiza N veces por segundo. Dominar estos números evita el 90% de los problemas de pantalla cortada, estirada o temblorosa.

Antes de operar cualquier equipo, hay que hablar el idioma del vídeo: qué es la resolución, qué es el frame rate, qué es progresivo y entrelazado, qué es la relación de aspecto, y por qué mezclar 50 Hz con 60 Hz da dolor de cabeza. Esta lección construye la base numérica que sustenta todo lo que viene después.

  • Píxel: La menor unidad de una imagen digital — un punto de color. La imagen es una matriz de píxeles (ancho x alto). Más píxeles = más detalle, más carga de procesamiento.
  • Resolución: Número de píxeles en horizontal x vertical. Estándares comunes: HD 1280x720, Full HD 1920x1080, 4K UHD 3840x2160. En LED, la resolución real depende de cuántos paneles unas (ej.: 10 paneles de 128x128 px = 1280 px de ancho).
  • Frame rate (tasa de cuadros): Cuántas imágenes por segundo se muestran: 24, 25, 30, 50, 60 fps. El cine es 24; la TV en Brasil históricamente 30/60 (estándar 60 Hz); Europa 25/50. Un frame rate más alto = movimiento más fluido.
  • Progresivo (p) vs Entrelazado (i): En progresivo (1080p) cada cuadro se dibuja entero. En entrelazado (1080i) cada cuadro se divide en dos campos (líneas impares y pares). Para LED y VJ, prefiere siempre progresivo — el entrelazado causa dentado en movimiento.
  • Relación de aspecto (aspect ratio): La proporción ancho:alto. 16:9 es el estándar de pantalla ancha. Los muros de LED a menudo tienen proporciones 'locas' (ej.: 21:9, 32:9 o paneles verticales 9:16) — necesitas producir contenido en la proporción exacta del muro para que no se distorsione.
  • Resolución nativa vs escalado: Cada display tiene una resolución nativa. Enviar una señal diferente obliga al equipo a reescalar (scaling), lo que añade latencia y puede difuminar. Lo ideal es siempre igualar la salida con la resolución nativa del destino.
  • Refresh rate en LED: A diferencia del frame rate del contenido, es la frecuencia con la que el panel de LED 'parpadea' internamente (ej.: 1920 Hz, 3840 Hz). Un refresh alto evita el efecto de scan lines (franjas negras) cuando la pantalla es filmada por una cámara.

Equipos / detalles

  • Generador de barras/test pattern: Equipo o software que genera patrones (barras SMPTE, cuadrícula, círculos) para verificar resolución, proporción y color de una pantalla antes del evento.
  • Scaler/procesador (ej.: Analog Way, Barco): Convierte una resolución de entrada a la nativa del destino, igualando el frame rate. Esencial cuando fuentes y pantallas no coinciden.

Paso a paso

  1. 1. Descubre la resolución nativa del destino: Cuenta los paneles de LED o lee la ficha del proyector/monitor. Ej.: pared de 12x6 cabinets de 168x168 px → 2016x1008 px.
  2. 2. Define la resolución de lienzo del contenido: Produce el contenido exactamente en esa resolución para mapear píxel a píxel, sin reescalado.
  3. 3. Fija un único frame rate: Elige 50 o 60 Hz para todo el evento y configura todas las fuentes y procesadores en él, evitando tearing y microcortes.
  4. 4. Verifica con un test pattern: Envía una cuadrícula y un círculo a la pantalla: si el círculo se convierte en elipse, la proporción está mal; si la cuadrícula no coincide con los bordes, hay reescalado.

En la práctica: Un cliente entrega un vídeo de apertura en 4K 24p, pero el muro es de 2016x1008 funcionando a 60 Hz.
